A CEO replaced their CMO twice in 18 months and got the same result both times.

Because the problem wasn't the CMO, it was the infrastructure.

You can't hire your way out of broken systems. The second CMO made the same requests as the first, got the same pushback, and left for the same reasons - broken attribution, inadequate data, and decisions driven by half-baked information.

Companies keep hiring new CMOs, hoping the next one will be better at Excel + gut feeling, while quietly setting them up to fail.

Gradually, word travels in marketing circles, and by the time you're hiring the third CMO, the candidate pool is limited to either people who are desperate, inexperienced, or don't know how to spot red flags.

The company thinks it's being selective, but the best candidates have already screened them out. You can't hire your way out of broken systems. You can only hire your way into a worse talent pool.

Outreach is valued at $4.4B.
Salesloft raised at valuation of $2.3B.
Apollo.io raised $100M at $1.6B valuation.

Everyone's racing to build AI agents that can write emails, make calls, and book meetings. But here's what nobody's talking about: the handoff problem.
An AI agent can craft a perfect outbound sequence. It can personalize at scale, follow up relentlessly, even book the meeting. But the moment that lead crosses from marketing to SDR to AE, context bleeds at every handoff.

This is the same problem that killed human-driven funnels. And we're about to recreate it with agents.

It's like the Mars Orbiter disaster. One team used metric units, another used imperial. Both were "right" locally. The spacecraft was lost because the boundary didn't share meaning. GTM teams repeat this pattern every quarter.

Marketing's MQL doesn't equal SDR's "accepted" doesn't equal Sales' "qualified." Small semantic drifts compound into pipeline chaos.

Now imagine that chaos multiplied by autonomous agents operating without institutional memory. The agentic era won't be won by the fastest agent builder. It'll be won by whoever solves context preservation across handoffs, human or machine.

We don’t lose many deals to competitors. We lose them to “we’ll build it ourselves.” And then we win them back.

I’ve seen the pattern enough times to stop arguing. Teams fall in love with the idea of building in-house because the prototype demo looks promising. What they don’t factor in is the ongoing, compounding cost, not just engineering hours but operational complexity.

Three months later, when the system breaks, it’s never just the tech. It’s everything around it: policies, governance, security, legal frameworks, pricing models. With AI, all of these evolve constantly.

Your prototype doesn’t stay a prototype. It becomes a second product, one that demands continuous updates just to remain viable.

“Build” works only when what you are building is the true differentiator. Most of the time, it is maintenance.

And maintenance compounds. Every new requirement, every pivot, every quick fix makes the system more fragile and more expensive to keep alive. By the time the tech is “working,” the business has already moved on.

If you still want to build, be explicit.
- What decisions will you be able to make when it is done?
- Who will own it long-term?
- Can you handle the operational overhead?

If you can’t answer those, don’t ship the prototype. Buy endurance, build edge.

"Credentials don't matter in AI" is lazy thinking. That narrative protects people from accountability.

The narrative goes, "A+ executives are failing at AI startups while 22-year-olds fine-tuning models in dorms are crushing it. Experience is dead!"

It's a half truth, and completely misdiagnosed. But let's think about who benefits from this narrative?

- The young builder who ships fast and breaks things? "Experience doesn't matter" explains why they don't need to understand enterprise complexity.
- The executive who failed at an AI startup? "The paradigm shifted" explains why their track record didn't translate.
- The investor who backed the wrong team? "Nobody could have predicted this" conveniently eliminates blame.

Everyone gets an excuse and nobody has to be wrong.

But then some executives are succeeding at AI companies, some young builders are failing. The pattern isn't "credentials vs. no credentials."

It's competence vs. incompetence, same as it ever was. The game has definitely changed, but competence still looks like competence:
- understanding problems deeply
- building solutions that work
- earning trust from customers.

“Credentials don’t matter” is comforting to people who don’t want to investigate why they failed. The uncomfortable truth is that in any era, some people figure it out and some don't. The reasons vary, but the outcome doesn't.

Chaos isn't a pit, chaos is a ladder.

And nowhere is that truer than in data. Software companies love to say “garbage in, garbage out.” It’s their evergreen excuse for why their product fails the moment they meet real enterprise data. But the truth is that real companies do not have clean data. They have a decade of system migrations, shortcuts, naming collisions, abandoned fields, accidental conventions, and improvised workarounds baked into every table.

Good data is a utopian concept, it's not because of intent, it's just because data is a high-entropy discipline. And entropy increases over time, meaning the messiness of your data is the natural state. Data doesn't stay clean, it degrades. Fields get misused, definitions drift, new systems get bolted on. Any system designed for a point-in-time snapshot of data quality will become obsolete as the data evolves.

This is why most enterprise AI implementations fail, they're built on fiction, that someone will "fix the data" before the system goes live. That never happens.
Products which can handle data chaos have an unfair advantage other those who assume perfection in data. Therefore, the only viable product (and pilot) design is one that assumes the mess and builds tolerance for it.

Your customer's customer is your REAL customer.

I got off a call where the team spent an hour discussing dashboards, not for the people on the call, but for a CXO who'd eventually see the dashboards and the reports. They cosplayed that CXO.

The person who signs your contract is rarely the person you need to impress. They're a conduit. Your real customer is their boss, their board, their cross-functional peers who can kill the deal or the renewal.

The CMO buying RevSure AI isn't buying for herself.
- She's buying for the board meeting where she needs to prove marketing ROI.
- She's buying for the CFO who questions every dollar.
- She's buying for the CEO who wants pipeline visibility.

I'm not selling to her, I'm arming her for battles I'll never see. If you don't understand the battles your champions are fighting, you're handing them a sword when they need a shield. Or vice versa.

The moment I started building for my customer's customer, our expansion revenue doubled. She alway had data, and information, but RevSure gave her the story that helped her fund campaigns which others wanted to kill, and even kill projects which were not moving the needle.

The real leverage is helping your champion win a room you’ll never be in.

No company dies from lack of data. They die when data becomes political instead of analytical.

A CMO told me this a while ago, "As soon as you present a slide that shows a different number than what sales sees, it becomes a debate about the numbers, not a conversation about what you're trying to do." The friction isn't about accuracy. It's about trust.

When the numbers don't match, teams don't fix the funnel & they fight over whose data is right. I call this the "Debate Tax."

And it compounds. Every unresolved dispute breeds more misalignment. Soon, teams stop bringing data to meetings altogether, because they know it'll be challenged. Eventually, the company flies blind by choice. That's more comfortable than fighting. Marketing stops trying to prove ROI because every attempt gets re-litigated.

The irony: the data existed all along. The fragmentation made it weaponizable. When every team has their own dashboard, every team has their own truth. And when everyone has their own truth, nobody has credibility.

This is why we built for the full funnel. When marketing, SDRs, and sales see the same number, the conversation shifts from 'whose data is right' to 'what do we do next.'

For 1,500 years, everyone knew the Earth was the center of the universe.

Ptolemy's geocentric model worked well enough, it predicted planetary positions. It fit the theology. And then Copernicus heliocentric model showed the sun was the center. His math was better & his predictions were more accurate.

His book got 'restricted' for 100+ years.

Moving from last-touch to multi-touch attribution is your organization's Copernican moment.

You're about to redistribute credit. Channels that looked dominant will shrink. Channels you were about to cut will show up everywhere. And people will fight it. Not because the data is wrong. Because the implications are uncomfortable.

- The head of paid media doesn't want to see organic getting credit.
- The events team doesn't want to share influence with email.
- The VP who championed the big rebrand doesn't want data showing it didn't move the needle.

Your first multi-touch report will be rejected. Plan for it.

- "The methodology must be wrong."
- "We should look at a different time period."
- "Can we rerun the analysis?"

This isn't failure, this is the process. Attribution doesn't fail because of bad math. It fails because executives would rather be comfortable than correct.

The Copernican Revolution wasn't rejected because the math was wrong. It was rejected because it was inconvenient.

Before you implement multi-touch, ask yourself: is your leadership ready to be wrong? If not, you're not buying attribution. You're buying expensive confirmation bias.

A CMO I know quit her job in 12 months of joining a series B startup.

She joined after spending 15 years at Big Tech, and one of the first things she tried to fix was their messy reporting data. Soon, she found herself questioning everything.

Someone who has never seen the power of data in sales of marketing funnel doesn't really know what they're missing. But someone who's lived in a hyper-regimented system for 15 years knows exactly what "good" looks like.

That awareness becomes a daily source of frustration. They fight for better systems for 12-18 months, and if nothing improves, they quit.

I keep seeing this - companies don't realize they're bleeding their most data-literate operators, the ones who could 10x their GTM motion. Instead, these leaders become the most expensive churn.

Listen to these leaders early, some of our implementations are led by such leaders who looked at RevSure AI as the bridge from chaos to clarity. Bet on their frustrations, or watch them walk away.

A CMO recently asked me: “What part of your Full-Funnel AI are you best at? Most startups only nail one thing.”

Fair question.

Most tools were built to solve tiny slices of the GTM motion — Visitors → leads, leads/Accounts → MQLs/MQLs, MQLs/MQAs → meetings, and so on.

That niche-first mindset is exactly why GTM is so fragmented today and precisely why most CMOs have no visibility into the effectiveness of their efforts!

My answer: “We’re best at the full funnel.”

Not because we try to be everything, but because we defined the problem differently.

We didn’t ask, “How do we optimize one stage?” We asked, “Why are these stages disconnected at all?”

I grew up in the world of complex enterprise supply chains, where the supply chain was one interconnected system: Demand → Inventory → Distribution → Production → Raw Materials.

And once you see GTM as a inter-connected system across Marketing, Sales, SDR/BDR, Partners, etc. something becomes obvious:

The biggest problems don’t live inside the stages.

They live between them.

That’s where handoffs break. That’s where insights die. That’s where revenue leaks.

RevSure AI exists to fix those interconnections, the part everyone else ignores.

So what are we best at?

Helping the entire GTM motion work as one system. That’s where the future of GTM is headed.

Investment in brand is like buying a chandelier, it lights up the entire room.

And Demand is the flashlight, it focuses on one specific spot. Without the chandelier, you're burning through flashlight batteries trying to illuminate the whole room, one corner at a time. That's expensive, exhausting & inefficient.

If you run demand gen without brand, every email is a cold intro. Every ad requires full context and every campaign starts from zero awareness. Your CAC goes through the roof because you're paying to educate prospects about who you are, what you do, and why they should care. Every. Single. Time.

But when brand does its job? The demand email gets opened because they've seen you before. The ad resonates because they already trust the category. The sales call is warm because your name isn't new.

So, brand makes demand cheaper. Not by magic, by doing the work upfront.

I used to think our biggest competitor was other attribution platforms. I was wrong.

Our biggest competitor is Excel + gut feeling.

Because adopting RevSure AI often means confronting three uncomfortable truths:
- Your intuition about what's working is probably wrong.
- Your flagship campaigns might be destroying value.
- You'll need to defund channels that powerful people care about.

That's not a technology decision. That's a political one.

For example: recently we onboarded a customer whose star campaign was creative resistance in their pipeline, not acceleration. Conversions fell by 50% for every lead that touched it. When we ran the incrementality test, the lift was negative 2.3x.

Marketing has optimized for the wrong signals for years, clicks instead of conversions, engagement instead of revenue, activity instead of outcomes.

Often, the “strategic” initiatives underperform by 5x compared to the small, unfunded experiments everyone ignored.

The encouraging shift I’ve seen over the last three years is that more CMOs are now embracing this complexity. They’re realizing the real unlock isn’t better attribution systems, it’s the courage to act when data contradicts instinct.

Here’s to the CMOs choosing truth over stories. You’re rebuilding how marketing earns trust.

The biggest threat to your data is not bad pipelines or models, it's scared people.

I was on a call yesterday with an analytics lead who said something that stunned me: "We normalize the data three times before showing it to the business."

Not because the data is inaccurate, because nobody trusts the raw truth anymore.

- First pass: make the raw data technically correct.
- Second pass: make it match what leadership expects to see.
- Third pass: translate it into something digestible.

By the time it reaches to leadership, the data has been filtered through three layers of institutional anxiety. Nobody remembers what the source actually said. They just know it's been "cleaned."

This is what happens when you optimize for comfort instead of truth. Someone, at some point, showed leadership raw data and got punished for it. Maybe it contradicted a narrative. Maybe it raised uncomfortable questions. Maybe it just looked messy.

So the team learned: don't show them reality. Show them a version of reality they can handle.

And now, years later, everyone's forgotten why they're doing it. They just know that "this is how we report here."

The cost? Nobody trusts the numbers anymore, because everyone knows they've been smoothed out. Adjusted & normalized.

You can't make good decisions on data you don't trust, and you can't trust data that's been filtered through fear.

CMOs love the phrase "invest more in what's working." It's usually wrong.

It sounds data-driven, it sounds smart, and here's why it's wrong:

Last year, a client asked: "How much should we spend on LinkedIn?"

Their data showed that $800K last quarter generated $12M in pipeline. ROI looked phenomenal.

Client: "Let's double it to $1.6M!"
Model: "You'll get $15M in pipeline."
Client: "Why not $24M?"
Me: "Because markets aren't infinitely scalable."

Long silence.

Here's the reality: You exhaust your best audience first. Then you move to lukewarm prospects. Then you're targeting people who'll never buy but happen to match your ICP on paper.

More spend doesn't mean proportional return. It means diminishing returns.

But here's the brutal part: They were already PAST optimal spend. They were at $800K. Optimal was $600K. They could have cut 25%, gotten 95% of the pipeline, and deployed that $200K somewhere with better ROI.

Instead, they "invested more in what's working."

Six months later, they'd tripled LinkedIn spend to $2.4M.
- Pipeline was down.
- ROI cratered.

The challenge isn't that CMOs are spending on the wrong channels, it's that they're overspending on the right ones.

The best marketing leaders don't just know what works. They know exactly how much to spend before returns collapse. "Double down on what's working" is great advice, until you hit diminishing returns. Then it's just expensive denial.

It’s like Bangalore traffic, the first few cars move fast, then everyone piles in, and suddenly nothing moves.

A customer of ours once decided to walk away from $1.8M, not because the data was wrong, but because the truth was inconvenient.

Our MMX model told them to cut $2M from LinkedIn and move it to Google. The data was obvious, the incrementality test was statistically significant, and the ROI difference was 4x.

But the decision wasn’t analytical. It was political. The ignored the suggestion, and only cut their budget by $200K, because "LinkedIn is strategic."

This is the pattern everywhere: Spend 6 months building the perfect attribution model. The model surfaces uncomfortable truths. And then the methodology gets questioned, not the spend.

We've created the world's most expensive validation engines. Because companies don't buy attribution to optimize. They buy it to validate.

I once showed a team their flagship PMAX campaigns had statistically significant negative lift. Meaning: they actively decreased conversions compared to doing nothing.

They asked if we could "rerun the analysis."

Attribution models don't fail because of bad math. They fail because of human courage.

The best GTM teams aren’t the ones with the prettiest dashboards.
They’re the ones willing to act on ugly truths, to kill campaigns that look good in board meetings but destroy value in reality.

So before you buy an attribution platform, ask yourself: are you looking for insights, or permission to keep doing what you're already doing?

Attribution is NOT a reporting project.

It’s a data and decision intelligence problem — one that demands rigor across every layer of the marketing analytics stack.

True attribution involves:

1) Data integration across fragmented systems
2) Harmonization to create a single, trusted source of truth
3) Measurement and analysis that reflect real buyer journeys
4) Validation to ensure accuracy and credibility
5) Insights that guide smarter actions
6) Visibility into what’s actually driving outcomes
7) Filtering and segmentation across hundreds of attributes and dimensions
8) And finally, forward-looking guidance that helps teams make better decisions.

Whenever a marketing team reduces attribution to a mere reporting exercise, it’s bound to deliver sub-optimal impact — limited to basic “justification” rather than optimization and growth.

Attribution done right isn’t about looking back — it’s about seeing clearly ahead.

We connected 7 data sources in less than 30 minutes.
Salesforce, Salesloft, Hubspot, LinkedIn, Google Ads, Facebook, and our web pixel.
That was day one with RevSure AI

What actually mattered? What came after.
Multiple working sessions per week with the team, a dedicated Slack channel, the same team every time - no handoffs.
We worked through every question together, from data hygiene to channel classification - white glove service from the start.

My favorite feature? The Journey Timeline.
We can now view hundreds, some times thousands of touch points that occurred before an opportunity even opened. From the account to the opportunity or just the primary contact view we were able to see each marketing and sales touch point that drove pipeline at Nasuni.

First & Last Touch serve their own purpose, but when you disregard everything in between you are missing a huge part of the story. A huge thanks to our fabulous RevSure team for helping us fill in the gaps on the full, collaborative journey between Marketing, Business Development, and Sales!

One touch point is never enough!

Inefficient handoffs and missing semantics once cost NASA $125M.

In 1998, the Mars Climate Orbiter was lost because of a unit mismatch. One team sent thruster data in pound-seconds. Another expected newton-seconds.

Both were “right” locally, but wrong globally. The boundary didn’t share meaning, so every course correction nudged the spacecraft slightly off. Over time, those small errors compounded. It entered the Martian atmosphere tens of kilometers too low and contact was lost on arrival day.

Every team had verified their part. None had validated the handoffs.

That’s the real lesson: It wasn’t a calculation error that doomed the Mars Orbiter. It was a handoff failure. GTM teams make the same mistake every quarter.

Marketing’s MQL, SDR’s Accepted, Sales’ Qualified - all are locally consistent, but semantically different. A lead becomes an opportunity without shared context.

These small drifts compound. And you end up missing your quarter.

Funnel definitions are your unit system in GTM funnel. Treat them as contracts, not suggestions. Preserve memory across handoffs. Make boundaries unambiguous.

The disaster never lives in the parts. It lives in the interfaces.

If everyone agrees on a transformation plan, it’s probably useless.

I used to get frustrated when every new stakeholder joined a sales or implementation call with a fresh objection.

"Buy vs build.," “Just another dashboard,” “What’s the ROI with you?”

It felt like resistance. Now I see it as a signal.

The accounts that started with the most objections often became our most successful implementations. Because those objections weren’t blockers, they were signals that we were touching something deep. It wasn’t just a software change, it was a change in how power and context flow inside the company.

Real transformation creates friction. It forces uncomfortable tradeoffs. If there’s no dissent, no resistance, no turf loss, then you’re just rearranging the org chart.

Consensus feels good, but it’s often the enemy of change. The initiatives that move smoothly rarely move the needle. The ones that start with debate, pushback, and discomfort are the ones that compound over time.

Because transformation isn’t about alignment. It’s about re-wiring how the system works, how data flows, how teams make decisions, how outcomes are measured. That’s bound to hurt before it helps.

If it goes down too smooth, it’s not deep enough.

“76% of NVIDIA employees are now millionaires.”
“1 in 3 has over $20 million.”

Every few weeks I see a post talking about it. And the conclusion people jump to is this:
“Equity builds ownership. Equity creates performance.”

That’s not causation, that’s correlation.

The best people I’ve worked with never needed equity to care. They cared first, and that’s why they earned equity later. If you took the same NVIDIA engineers and dropped them into another company, they’d still out-perform. Because it’s not the stock options that made them world-class, it’s their craft, their drive, their obsession with building great things.

We cherry-pick narratives that suit us. We want to believe ESOPs create excellence, because it’s convenient, for founders asking for buy-in, or employees asking for upside.

But no one really wants to talk about the other side. Jensen Huang openly says he “tortures” his people to greatness, and they accept it because they believe in the mission. If any other CEO said that, the internet would cancel them overnight.

The truth is, compensation follows culture, not the other way around. Equity didn’t create NVIDIA’s culture of ownership, that culture created the kind of people who deserved equity.

So next time you hear, “everyone at NVIDIA is a millionaire,” remember: that’s the result, not the reason.

Many marketers I meet carry “attribution trauma.”

- They’ve tried last-click.
- They’ve tried multi-touch.
- They’ve tried custom ML models.

Each time, the model created more debate than clarity. So now, some teams swing to the other extreme: “Let’s not measure at all. Let’s just do good marketing.”

I get it. I’ve been working with marketing teams for 20+ years, across Fortune 1000 B2B and B2C. And here’s what I’ve learned:

Doing good marketing and measuring good marketing are not in opposition. They’re complementary.

No CEO will accept “we don’t measure.” And frankly, neither should marketing leaders.

The answer isn’t avoidance. It’s building the right measurement framework:

- Contextual to your product, business model, and GTM motion
- Owned by marketing (with support from ops, data, and analytics)
- Evolving in quality over time so decisions improve, not just reports

Because in the end, measurement isn’t a policing mechanism. It’s a learning mechanism. The better you measure, the faster you can double down on what works, cut what doesn’t, and build trust across the org.

If marketing doesn’t own this, someone else will. And that’s rarely a good outcome.

Don’t let past attribution scars push you into avoidance. Build the framework that works for your business - before it’s too late.

Building everything your customers ask for is the fastest way to become irrelevant.

Customer obsession isn’t about saying yes to every request. It’s about asking why the request exists in the first place.

I’ve had customers ask for more dashboards, more reports, more filters. On the surface, that sounds reasonable. But if you keep delivering exactly that, you don’t create clarity, you create clutter and feature bloat. You end up creating reports which nobody reads, and funnels which leak context.

The noise grows, but the answers don’t.

The real friction was never the lack of dashboards or fields. It was that every team saw the funnel in fragments. Marketing saw campaigns, SDRs saw leads and Sales saw opportunities. But no one saw the whole story end to end.

So we solved for the system, not the surface. A single funnel with shared memory, where every touchpoint, every handoff, every next-best action is visible across teams. Suddenly, the original requests disappear. Nobody asks for “just one more dashboard.” They ask, “What’s the fastest path to conversion given everything we already know about this account?”

Because in the end, no one wakes up wanting another feature, but they do wake up thinking about their jobs, and getting better at them. And if you deliver on that, you become the upgrade to how they work, not just hat they work with.

EQ has become an excuse to avoid hard conversations.

Does high EQ help? 100%. But we have confused EQ with:
- sugarcoating the truth
- sandwiching critical feedbacks between two compliments
- being overly nice, even when it doesn’t help.

Basically, it has been watered down into politeness theater.

But real EQ is the opposite. It’s having the courage to be direct, the empathy to deliver it with care, and the commitment to help someone grow, even when it’s uncomfortable.

Remember - candour without care is brutal, and care without candour sets up people for failure.

The most challenging and intense stint I had was at Mu Sigma Inc. I couldn't find time for my family back then, we had super intense arguments in our teams. But the core was that the leadership wanted us to win.

I want the same for my team at RevSure AI, they should win, learn and when they look back after 5-10 years, they should look at their stint here as their most intense and rewarding stint. I am committed to their win, and I believe that is the real definition of EQ - building a culture where failures are accepted, wins are rewarded, candour is appreciated and everyone pushes each other to bring out best in us.

Because when you really care about your team, you don’t protect them from hard truths, you prepare them to win.

We think buyers want speed. But what they’re really paying for is certainty.

Last month, I was on a call with a CMO and she mentioned that she wanted to know if she's going to miss her pipeline target.

Now, her team had dozens of platforms already for intent, ABM, ads, SDR cadences. And they weren’t slow, they were noisy.

What was missing was a single model that could say: Today is day 67 of the quarter, here’s the $17M in pipe you’ve already generated, here’s the $3.6M likely to convert from in-funnel leads, and here’s the $4M you still need demand to create.

That certainty killed anxiety, it turned guessing into planning. And that's your real moat, replace uncertainty with clarity. Because no CXO craves for faster dashboards, they crave for predictable outcomes.

So the next time you build tools or dashboards, ask yourself: are you helping your buyers move faster, or are you giving them the confidence to know exactly where they stand? The answer will define your real competitive advantage.

Build vs Buy is not a technology debate.

It's a timeline debate. And I keep coming across this classic line, "We can build this in-house."

Of course, you can. You can also cook every meal at home, but if your team is flying blind, choosing the "we'll build" option is like promising to learn how to cook after you've already invited 20 guests over.

We had this exact discussion with a CTO and CMO at a large enterprise. The CTO was excited about building. The CMO cut in: “I don’t have visibility now. So, I need a solution that works NOW.”

That urgency is often underestimated. I’ve observed CMOs come and go in the same company because they waited too long for their support teams to figure out how to get them the right visibility, insights and recommendations to course correct and refine their marketing efforts.

Yes, given enough engineers and enough quarters, companies can build anything. But when the cost of waiting outweighs the cost of buying, the debate is already settled.

Buying is not a shortcut, it’s your hallway pass to “time to truth.”

PS: Here are the builders of RevSure AI who are obsessed with powering the CMO's journey to the truth.

Enterprise software doesn’t reward noise.

Everyone’s talking about Oracle’s $240B stock jump in a single day. But that wasn’t about one quarter. It was the result of years of slow, boring work that built a order backlog so massive Wall Street couldn’t ignore it.

In enterprise AI, the number that changes your story isn’t this quarter’s revenue. It’s the already‑won, not‑yet‑recognized demand sitting in your pipeline as signed commitments. That’s the “we already chose you number”. When it compounds, the market stops arguing with your narrative and starts repricing your company.

And you don’t get there by stacking features or chasing bottom up dev and social media mindshare. You get there by saying yes to the hard, unglamorous asks that actually unblock deployment: capacity planning, sovereign options, messy integrations, predictable SLAs, Security, Compliance, multi-cloud realities.

AI doesn’t replace the unglamorous work, but it surely makes the payoffs much bigger.

Most PMs today aren’t really doing product management.

They’re managing delivery, coordinating stand-ups, copying competitor UIs, and chasing JIRA tickets.

The craft of understanding behaviour, building around psychology, and shaping intuition, has been fading for a while.

Part of this was inevitable. PM became a cool, “mini-CEO” job. It attracted people who were good at managing processes, not necessarily at building products.

But this era is about to end. Because now PMs aren’t just designing for humans. They’re designing for agents. Humans forgive sloppy design. If a filter isn’t obvious on a dashboard, a user will click around until they find it. They bring intuition to the table.

But an AI agent won’t assume. It won’t "intuitively" explore. They’ll only act on the context you encode.

Which means PMs will have to go back to first principles, designing context-rich environments, defining success unambiguously, and building systems where ambiguity equals failure.

This shift will expose mediocrity quickly. Teams will get leaner. And only the PMs who truly understand product thinking, not delivery timelines, will thrive in the age of agents.

Everyone loves to say a deal “came inbound.”

But inbound is rarely the start. It’s usually the last mile, after months of nudges, touch-points, and those “Oh, you again” moments.

For example: one of our recent wins didn’t begin with a cold email. A VP of Marketing filled our demo form only after:
- Hearing about us at a portfolio conference
- His colleague had already seen our content on LinkedIn
- And their CMO was already pushing the ROI question internally.

By the time they reached us, the ground was already prepared.
Events → warm references → inbound request → rigorous due diligence.

It looked like serendipity, but serendipity is rarely luck. It’s earned surface area. The awkward panels, the half-read LinkedIn posts, the reputation that makes your prospect curious enough to reach out.

Inbound isn’t magic. It’s persistence compounding until one day your buyer thinks, “Fine, let’s book the demo.”

Back in 2000, I was working in supply chain planning for retailers across the US and Europe.

And this was the time, when the world wasn't as hyper-connected as it's today. So, every detail mattered, a missing spec or unclear instruction could delay shipments, halt assembly lines, or create costly detours.

Additionally, no action could be taken in silos. A tweak in Barcelona might impact the hypermarket in Boston. The only way to run business was being obsessive about context.

However, as we got more connected, tribal knowledge started to fill for context gaps. You could always pick up the phone or send a message and learn from osmosis. So, one could survive even with half baked signals by asking around.

That’s how most software and enterprise sales still run: half‑baked signals stitched together by smart people who “just know.”

But Agents don't "just know."

They don’t have hallway chats, institutional memory, or politics translators. Give them fuzzy stages, inconsistent definitions (“influence” vs. “acceleration”), or undocumented hand‑offs, and they stall.

That’s why early agentic wins cluster in narrow, vertical workflows - coding, GTM ops, support triage, where context can be contained. But agents will soon outnumber humans across more complex operations. When that happens, what worked for supply chain becomes mandatory for everyone: context engineering - one taxonomy, crisp stage definitions, contract‑like hand‑offs, shared memory that survives reorgs.

The challenge ahead isn’t just building smarter models, It’s the system around it. If you don’t engineer context, your agents will engineer chaos.

Burnout rarely happens because someone has too much work.

In fact, the top performers I’ve worked with thrive when they’re challenged. They crave responsibility. They’re energized by tough problems, fast pace, and real ownership. That’s where they come alive.

When they do hit a wall, it’s usually not the workload, it’s the environment.

It’s unnecessary bureaucracy. It’s back-to-back meetings with no value. It’s leaders who mistake busyness for results. It’s a culture that slows things down, buries purpose, and isolates excellence.

They don’t burn out from intensity. They burn out from nonsense.

No one’s ever said, “I loved my manager because they protected me from hard work.” What they remember is the leader who removed the friction, who made meaningful work possible.

Create a culture that amplifies impact, and your best people won’t slow down, they’ll double down. Not out of obligation, but because they want to.

I used to run QBRs like a news anchor.

Prepared, polished, on script… and completely forgettable.

I’d walk in with the perfect deck with usage stats, trend lines, roadmap updates. Our customers nodded along politely. The driven ones extracted most value from our product and the others required constant pushing.

That’s when I flipped the format. Instead of 'performing' on what we had delivered, I started asking for what we needed from them.

The change was immediate.

POCs turned into multi-year contracts, because champions started pulling us deeper into their ecosystem, teaching us how to read their data, how to navigate their org, how to unlock adoption faster.

A QBR is the one moment you have every stakeholder’s attention, decision-makers, champions, skeptics, at the same table. Don’t waste it reading slides, use it to shape the path forward:

- “Here’s where adoption could stall, and what we’ll need from you.”
- “Here’s the expansion motion, and the executive air cover required.”
- “Here’s the blocker we can’t solve without your sponsorship.”

When you flip a QBR from updates to asks, you stop acting like a vendor and start operating like a partner. And that’s when customers lean in, because what they want most is to help you help them succeed.

Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Jerry Henry, E.T. Mac, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Tom Wentworth, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Shillinglaw, Vivek Gautam, Shantanu, Jayaram

In the early days of a startup, founders are the concierges of their startups. They know every prospect by name, remember which role each candidate is applying for, and follow up with every customer personally. Nothing slips, because everything is existential.

Every lead, every hire, every dollar has a human guardian.

But as the company scales, dashboards take over. Funnels, stages, and scores replace relationships. GTM motions become process-driven. Efficiency improves, but empathy evaporates.

And this is where things start to break.

Because you can't empathize with a dashboard. They don't feel friction, they don’t notice the hesitation in a prospect’s voice and they can’t tell you why a deal actually went cold.

Take marketing. Today, they measure everything, but experience almost nothing. It's the classic Goodhart’s Law: when metrics become targets, the system loses meaning.

If GTM leaders shadowed their own leads, even for a week, they’d redesign their funnels. They’d fix scoring, stop irrelevant sequences, and see the dead zones, where buyer intent dies from silence.

This empathy gap in modern GTM can be closed by AI Agents. Imagine if every lead had a guardian again, this time, powered by AI. One that could:
- Carries context across handoffs
- Detects silences and suggests next best actions
- Tunes sequences in real time
- Tracks what actually moves deals forward

When you close the empathy gap, everything improves. Better data, meaningful stages, trustworthy scoring, smarter campaigns, and outreach that respects the buyer journey.

The future of GTM isn’t better reporting, it’s scaling empathy with guardians for every lead.

Sam Altman just declared, "We’re entering the “fast-fashion era of SaaS."

And now, we're all about to hear a lot of “premium SaaS is over” takes on LinkedIn and Twitter.

I don’t buy it.

AI makes it effortless to spin up an app. That’s perfect for experiments and low-stakes internal tools. But the second your “quick build” touches pipeline, audits, PII, stage transitions, attribution, or anything you’ll defend in a board deck, fast becomes fragile.

Here’s what I keep seeing:
- Team hacks together an AI app. Flashy demo.
- Two quarters of babysitting: re-prompting, re-indexing, patching edge cases, arguing with security, chasing schema drift.
- Original builder leaves. And the “cheap” tool becomes the most expensive thing in the stack.

Enterprise GTM is not consumer tech. Demos don’t carry you into year two. Context, integrations, governance, support, and boring reliability do. That’s why vertical, opinionated platforms keep winning, because they’ve already absorbed the thousands of messy realities (regions, deal sizes, policy exceptions, data residency, who’s allowed to see what, and why).

Use fast fashion where the blast radius is small. For the motions your revenue and reputation depend on, buy the well-tailored suit.

Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Jerry Henry, E.T. Mac, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Tom Wentworth, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Sh

Great products are born not in harmony, but in constructive tension, between two people who violently disagree and deeply respect each other.

I believe in hard‑wiring constructive conflict - one owner for craft (clarity, intuitiveness, end‑user love), one owner for depth (rapid experiments, funnels, data engineering). And both of them carry real authority.

Their job isn’t to “win” individually, but to produce the third answer neither would have found alone.

Take design. We’ve put Vivek in charge of our design function. His mission is clear, understand the end user, listen to our solution engineers, data engineers, and GTM teams, and then design interfaces that are intuitive, usable, and loved.

In most orgs, design loses its independent voice as it rolls into product or marketing, and it ends up getting shaped by product or marketing moulds.

But I want our design function to challenge both.
- To ask what the user truly wants, not just what fits the roadmap.
- To fight for clarity without compromising on product depth.
- And to be challenged back, so simplicity doesn’t come at the cost of depth.

This constructive tension between them has built RevSure AI a very deep but still very intuitive product.

I believe when craft dominates, you get art with no adoption. When product or growth dominates, you get numbers with no soul. And when the tension is designed in, you get durable velocity.

Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Jerry Henry, E.T. Mac, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Tom Wentworth, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Shillinglaw

Most AI product implementations don’t fail because of the product.

They fail because of a change in point of contact or the internal champion. We often blame internal power dynamics, but the real root cause is something deeper: a lack of context.

- Why was this product bought in the first place?
- How is success being measured?
- Is the problem even big enough to solve?

Without that clarity, momentum dies.

This is why AI companies need to evolve from a product-first mindset to a solution-first approach. It’s not just about what you’ve built; it’s about how well you understand the customer's world. That means acting less like vendors and more like consultants, leading with context rather than features.

I saw this up close at Mu Sigma, and it’s exactly why, at RevSure AI, we built our GTM and CSM teams with marketers. These are people who’ve lived the problem we’re solving. They’re best positioned to act as consultants and handhold implementations, not just sell and exit.

And while “deployment-as-a-service” is now trending, we’ve operated that way from day one. Our solution architects and data platform team have always worked shoulder to shoulder with enterprise teams, understanding workflows, tech stacks, integration hurdles, and even organizational dynamics.

You can’t expect enterprise adoption without first earning the right to influence. And that starts with understanding their context. This is why I believe CS and FDE teams will play an increasingly critical role in AI-native companies, something Jason M. Lemkin highlighted some time back.

Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Jerry Henry, E.T. Mac, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Tom Wentworth, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Shillinglaw

We just wrapped up our biggest quarter, for the second time in a row.

That’s two back-to-back record-breaking quarters, and our GTM team continues to raise the bar every time. We’ve already grown 2.4x this year and 4.8x over the last 12 months. Our quarterly Net New ARR is up 10x, and the momentum we’ve built puts us in a strong position for the months ahead.

But the most exciting part isn’t just the growth. It’s how we’re expanding our customers’ vision.

In the last six months, attribution has evolved from a reporting mechanism into a strategic decision-making tool. Our customers are now using it to drive forward-looking actions, not just measure past performance.

And that shift is paying off. Some of our newest acquisitions are choosing us at a 50% premium over other players because of the depth of our platform and the outcomes we deliver.

RevSure AI, Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Jerry Henry, E.T. Mac, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Tom Wentworth, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Shillinglaw

Here's what we heard unanimously last week from many solid operators and startup founders:

“What you're building is seriously strong.”
“Everyone needs this, why wait 3 months of end of quarter to find out if marketing dollars were spent right?”
“Can RevSure AI really do what you say it does?”

Alex and I had an opportunity to join Operator Collective’s second Product Showcase Week, and as expected, when you bring together that many builders, operators, and doers in one place, the energy gets contagious.

When I started building RevSure, I knew one thing for sure: a weekend-hacked, vibe-coded product wouldn't cut it.

In today’s enterprise landscape, consolidation is the theme, and most companies are in deep build-vs-buy debates. So, you need to build a really solid product with a very strong business case for enterprises to buy your product.

And honestly, a lot of what we do at RevSure is solving the unsexy but critical stuff: data harmonization, connectivity, stitching insights together across the funnel. It’s not glamorous, but if you don’t solve this, you’re just building something a smart internal developer could ship in 30 days.

That’s what drove us to build RevSure: an intense and challenging (but now very rewarding) journey to help enterprises make better & faster GTM decisions.

Also, major shoutout to Mallun Yen and team for not just curating an incredible room of operators, but building a fund around that operator DNA.

Capital is a commodity and most advice isn’t context-aware. What matters today, especially for startup founders, is sponsorship and distribution. And Operator Collective is nailing that for its portfolio.

Operator Collective 🔆, Mallun Yen, Haley Brannan, Caroline Caswell

founder-data\\Deepinder_LinkedIn_Pack_v1.text:
Built per the Viral Post Adaptation Framework v5 + Opening Line Amplifier v5.

10 viral source posts → 10 nine-post packs in Deepinder's voice → 90 finished posts.

Each post carries: final draft, opener diagnosis, 3 amplifier versions, mode (W/D), recency-feed mapping, and gate audit at pack level.

- Founder corpus: built from public LinkedIn + X + Eternal earnings + interviews. Not a private corpus.

- Argument matrix (15 takes), recency feed (10 items), exclusions, wrestling/declaring ratio: built from public sources.

- A pass with the founder's own argument matrix, exclusions file, and recency feed will be sharper.

  Batch A (3 mirrored): A1, A2, A3 — keep source's beat order, founder's voice on a different argument

  Batch B (6 mechanics-only): B1-B6 — same psychological move, different surface every time

  Wrestling/Declaring split: 4W / 5D per pack (founder's 50/50 ratio, slight wrestling lean)

  - Diagnosis (5 gates: mechanic, plausibility, coherence, specificity, mode-preservation)

  - 3 candidate openers labelled with mechanic, mode, plausibility, coherence, specificity, rating

  Several recency items appear across multiple packs because the recency feed has 10 items and the wrestling slots across 10 packs total ~40-45 — collisions are mathematically expected.

  Per-post diagnostic notes flag where a topic recurs. Do NOT ship two posts on the same recency item in the same week or quarter — see the Pack Convergence sheet for a deployment schedule.

  Founder Context     — fact list, argument matrix, recency feed, exclusions, voice markers, ratio

  Source 1..Source 10 — one tab per pack with 9 posts + amplifier work + gate audit

  Pack Convergence    — cross-pack overlap detection and recommended ship sequence

  - Verify a fact you didn't put on the public-fact list. Founder should sanity-check every named number/event before publishing.

  - Replace the founder's own recency feed. Three of the wrestling clusters (stepping-down letter, earnings-call analyst Q, Newslaundry critique) are the framework's best read of his current public preoccupations — replace with his actual list before next pack.

  - Decide his exclusions file. Update before next cycle.

Marketer apology — sarcastic apology + listing mechanic

Sarcastic apology + indented-list of intruders + 'you came in on Monday with a plan' anaphora + 'and your job somehow became managing that' closer.

Lost judgment in product building — measured contrarian thesis

Calm contrarian thesis ('we have lost judgment') + 5 numbered counterbalances + closing axiom.

VP of Marketing writing blog posts — bait headline + structural failure

Bait headline (capitalised statement of failure) + scenario + named structural concept ('seniority inversion') + economics + 3-step fix + close.

It's getting weird out here — chaos opener + pundit roast

Chaos opener + listing of overconfident pundits + 'nobody knows what's going on' + personal aside + closing call to action.

'Not knowing how to code is an advantage' is nonsense — direct contrarian declaration

Direct contrarian declaration + grounded counter-evidence + list of what to learn + close.

'Each dot is 3.2M people' — visual scale anchor + AI-adoption reveal

Visual hook + math-anchored stack (% of users who do X) + scale reveal + 'we're at the start' framing.

'2023 Hype: ... Reality: ...' — year-stack with ironic punchline

Year-stacked hype escalation + flat reality punchline.

Conway's Law — 'every product mirrors the org that built it'

Universal-truth opener + historical anchor (Conway 1968) + structural reframe + 'pull up your org chart' close.

CMO board meeting / negotiables — second-party verdict + tradeoff framing

Second-party verdict ('best thing I heard') + planning meeting scene + clean tradeoff statement + close on imperative.

Sam Altman / Slack — borrowed authority + first-person + critique-of-tools

Borrowed-authority quote + first-person 'I live this' + tool critique + 'death spiral' mechanism + simple personal system + close.

Founder & Vice Chairman, Eternal (formerly Zomato). Founder, Continue Research / Temple.

Public Facts (every named entity in any post traces here)

Stepped down as Group CEO of Eternal on 1 February 2026; Albinder Singh Dhindsa (ex-Blinkit CEO) took over.

Public reason for stepping down: pursuing 'higher-risk exploration and experimentation' outside a public company.

Stated quote in investor letter: 'I want Eternal to become India's most valuable company. I want us to serve a billion customers.'

Q3 FY26 revenue: Rs 16,315 cr (up 202% YoY); profit: Rs 102 cr (up 73%).

Q2 FY26 adjusted revenue: Rs 13,968 cr (172% YoY). Zomato profit crossed Rs 500 cr that quarter (5.3% margin on NOV — highest ever).

Q1 FY26: Revenue Rs 7,167 cr (+70.4% YoY); profit Rs 25 cr (-90% YoY) due to Blinkit dark-store reinvestment.

Blinkit added 272 stores in Q2 FY26 alone, total store count ~1,816.

Founded Continue Research with $25M of personal capital (Oct/Nov 2025) for longevity science.

Building Temple — a wearable that tracks cerebral blood flow at the temple region. Has personally worn it >1 year.

Authored the Gravity Aging Hypothesis (Nov 2025) — open-source paper on cerebral blood-flow decline driven by upright posture.

Worked one-day shifts as a Zomato delivery partner himself; wrote about it publicly.

Wrote the controversial 'Chief of Staff' hiring post in Dec 2024 (Rs 0 salary, Rs 20L donation requirement) — over 2,190 comments.

Posted a 12-paragraph thread on 2 Jan 2026 about the gig economy making the labor of the poor visible to the rich for the first time.

Public X handle: @deepigoyal. Often opens posts with 'I'm not sharing this as the CEO of Eternal, but as a fellow human...'

AM01: Class invisibility was the hidden moat of pre-gig capitalism; the gig economy made the poor visible.

AM02: Customers want global standards at local prices — the unique gravity well of Indian consumer business.

AM03: Habit, not love, is the actual moat in consumer products — and habit is built one boring quarter at a time.

AM04: Quick commerce is a real-estate density problem dressed up as a delivery problem.

AM05: Profit and growth are not opposites at scale — restraint compounds.

AM06: Founders should write in public to think clearly, not to grow followers.

AM07: Stepping down from operator roles is harder than stepping up — sunk-cost identity is the hardest cost.

AM08: The next decade is biology + compute, not just compute.

AM09: Over-staffing kills founder-led companies faster than under-staffing.

AM10: Public-company CEO incentives push founders away from the experiments that built the company in the first place.

AM11: Open-sourcing your own thinking compounds because peers get to disagree publicly.

AM12: 10-minute delivery is misframed — the real question is whether instant is structural or subsidised.

AM13: Boring discipline beats heroic decisions — the 5.3% margin came from 100 small no's, not one big yes.

AM14: Indian consumer founders should hire for distance from comfort, not distance from elite credentials.

AM15: The frontier of the next billion users is not yet online — most planning models are dangerously blind to that.

Recency Feed — last 30-60 days (wrestling-mode posts draw from here)

RF01: What it actually felt like to write the stepping-down letter — and how long he sat with the decision.

RF02: The first calendar week post-CEO — what conversations stopped happening, what conversations finally could.

RF03: Watching Blinkit cross Zomato in NOV — and what it tells him about category timing he didn't see at the time.

RF04: Pushback after the gig-economy thread — what the critic (Newslaundry) got right.

RF05: Gravity Aging Hypothesis pushback from neuroscientists; why he chose to publish before peer review.

RF06: A specific Temple data point from his year of wearing it that surprised him.

RF07: Watching Albinder make a call he'd have made differently — and choosing not to override.

RF08: Q3 FY26 earnings call — an analyst question he didn't have a clean answer for.

RF09: A delivery partner conversation about something the rider wanted that nobody had asked him for.

RF10: Realising his own LinkedIn writing has become more declarative since stepping down — and what that signals.

Don't restate the Chief of Staff post or its terms.

Don't name Swiggy, Zepto, Instamart, Bigbasket as competitors unless quoting a public stat.

Don't quote specific board members or named investors.

Don't disclose any internal KPI not already in public earnings.

Don't quote specific customer names without public attribution.

Don't manufacture updates he hasn't publicly had — wrestling content draws from RF only.

Mixes 'I' and 'we' deliberately — 'I' for personal stake, 'we' for company decisions.

Opens with a flat declarative or confession; rarely a question.

Prose over bullets; bullets only for concrete enumerations.

One-word paragraph for emphasis ('Always.', 'Gone.', 'Once.').

Closes on a quiet admission or specific physical detail — almost never on a moral or CTA.

Will write 'I'm not sharing this as a CEO, but as a...' to flag personal mode.

Comfortable with contradictions; sits in tension rather than resolving cleanly.

Uses public earnings numbers; doesn't invent dollar amounts.

Names physical scenes — dark stores, riders, malls, podcast studios, his own desk.

10-minute delivery is a real-estate question, not a velocity question — stop debating speed.

I want to apologise, to every quick-commerce operator whose roadmap changed this morning because someone with no operating experience and a lot of opinions had a thought over the weekend.

> The journalist read a thread on a flight.
> The investor's friend timed an order in Bandra.
> The think-tank person quoted a Goldman note from London.
> The columnist wrote a 1,200-word piece on whether ten minutes is too fast.
> The LinkedIn voice with eighty thousand followers asked if it's even sustainable.
> Someone's cousin in Gurgaon got a damaged sachet and wrote a Reddit post.

You came in on Monday with a plan.
A boring plan.
A plan that was working — store density up, on-time up, refunds down.
And by 10 a.m. the entire executive team was discussing whether ten minutes is morally defensible.

Not because the catchment changed.
Not because the unit economics moved.
Not because a rider called you and said this is unsafe.

Because someone with no operating experience and a lot of opinions had a thought over the weekend.

And your job somehow became managing that conversation instead of the eleven pin-codes still under-served by the network.

The fight isn't about ten minutes. It never was. It's about whether your dark stores are dense enough that ten minutes is just what falls out of the math. If they are, the speed is a side-effect. If they aren't, no amount of rider pressure makes the number real.

Density is the question. Speed is the souvenir.

CEO incentives push founders away from experimentation

Public-company titles slowly rewrite the questions a founder is willing to ask in public.

I want to apologise, to the version of me who used to write longer, weirder, less-polished posts on this app.

For two years I told myself the writing got cleaner because I was getting clearer.
It got cleaner because I was getting careful.

You can see it in the drafts folder.
The half-finished thread about why our Q1 profit fell 90 percent — never shipped.
The note about a Blinkit catchment we got wrong — never shipped.
The piece about what I actually thought when I read the analyst question I couldn't answer last quarter — never shipped.

Each time I went back to the draft, the public-company CEO in my head edited the founder out of it.
Eight quarters of that and the founder voice is gone. The CEO voice is what's left.

I noticed it the week after I stepped away. I sat down to write something on Temple and the first paragraph was a press release. I had to delete it twice before the writing sounded like a person.

This is one of the costs nobody warns founders about when their company goes public. Not the regulation, not the analyst pressure, not the share price. The slow rewrite of which questions you're willing to ask in public.

I'm still figuring out what to do with this. Part of me wants to publish the half-finished drafts as-is. Part of me knows they'll be misread the second they go up. The honest answer is I haven't decided.

This post is me trying to remember which voice was mine.

We're paying for the global-product expectation in margin, and the public conversation pretends that's a strategy bug.

I want to apologise, to every Indian consumer founder who got asked again this week why their margins are 'so low.'

> The international fund analyst who benchmarks you against a US comp.
> The PE associate who plugs your AOV into a Western model and gasps.
> The journalist who calls a four percent margin a 'profitability concern.'
> The twitter thread that compares a Mumbai grocery basket to a Whole Foods basket.
> The board meeting where someone says 'why can't we be more like Costco.'

You came into the year with a plan.
Charge a hundred and forty rupees for a delivery your global comp charges nine dollars for.
Move four million orders a day on infrastructure that costs a quarter of theirs.
And still hit a margin that lets you keep building.

It's not a strategy gap. It is the gravity of the market you chose. The Indian consumer wants the same product the consumer in San Francisco wants and is willing to pay one-sixth the price for it. That gap doesn't close because you wrote a deck. It closes because you ran ten thousand small experiments to take cost out without taking trust out.

That work doesn't show up as a single big number on a slide. It shows up as a margin that improves a few basis points a quarter for years. The international comp gets to skip that part. We don't.

Stop apologising for the gravity. Build for it.

User love decays in a quarter; habit is the only consumer moat that compounds.

A friend who runs a consumer brand asked me last week what I think the real moat is for a category leader.

I told him the truthful answer, which is the boring one. It's habit. It's not love. It is never love.

Love is what your customer says about you in a podcast. Habit is what they do when their thumb is on autopilot at 9:47 p.m. and dinner has not been thought about. Love costs nothing. Habit costs years.

You build habit one quarter at a time. You ship the order on time when the rain is bad. You refund without arguing when the packet is wrong. You don't sell their data when an investor suggests it. You don't change the home screen because someone in growth has a thesis. You don't add a fee because the unit economics are tight this month. You absorb. You absorb. You absorb.

And one day a customer opens your app without thinking, the way they open the door of their own house, and you have a business.

Most consumer founders I see right now are optimising for love. They want a screenshot. They want a tweet. They want the word delight in a Google review.

That is not the moat. That is the marketing of the moat.

The moat is the night the app opens before the customer remembers why.

The hardest cost of leaving an operator role is the identity that built it — not the calendar or the title.

It took me four months to write the letter announcing I was stepping away as CEO.

Not because the decision was hard. The decision was clear by August.

What was hard was that I couldn't figure out the first sentence. Every draft started with the company. 'Eternal has had a strong year.' 'Eternal is at an inflection point.' Eternal, Eternal, Eternal.

None of that was the real opening. The real opening was that I had spent eighteen years being a person whose first sentence was the company. And I didn't know how to write a sentence as someone else.

I think this is what people miss when they talk about founder transitions. They talk about timing, succession, valuation, board dynamics. The actual cost is the part nobody puts on a slide — the rewrite of the sentence you start with when you introduce yourself.

I'm two months in now. I still occasionally say 'we' about a Q4 decision I had no role in. Albinder is making calls I would have made differently and choosing to learn that this is fine.

The work to be done is not on the calendar. The calendar emptied out exactly when I expected it to. The work is on the sentence.

I haven't found mine yet.

An analyst question I couldn't answer made me realise our 5.3% margin came from saying no, not yes.

On the Q3 earnings call last quarter an analyst asked me a question I didn't have a clean answer for.

He asked which single decision drove the 5.3 percent margin on food delivery — the highest in our history.

I gave him the standard reply about category maturity and operating leverage. He nodded. The line moved on.

But I sat with the question for the next two weeks. Because the truth was: there wasn't a single decision. There were about a hundred small no's spread over six quarters. No to the new fee structure that would have lifted take-rate by forty basis points. No to the loyalty programme our growth team had a beautiful deck for. No to the second courier integration we kept being told would 'unlock' something. No to a category expansion an investor told me was 'obvious.' No, no, no.

None of those no's were heroic. Most of them I forgot inside a week. They added up the way compound interest adds up — invisibly, until somebody asks you on a call to point at the moment.

What I should have said to him: there is no moment. The margin is the residue of a hundred meetings where we chose to be boring.

I'm still annoyed I didn't say that on the call.

The quick commerce business is a leasing business with a delivery layer on top.

Most of the quick-commerce conversation in India still treats it as a delivery problem.

It is not.

It is a real-estate problem with a delivery skin stretched over the top. The thing that decides whether you make money in a pin code is not the rider's speed. It is whether you have a 1,800-square-foot box within 1.6 kilometres of enough households at the right basket size. The rider is downstream of that math.

Blinkit added 272 stores in one quarter and the network crossed 1,800. Every one of those is a lease, a landlord, a power approval, a fire NOC, a back-room layout, a shrinkage profile. Most of the operational work in this category looks nothing like a tech business and everything like a retail real-estate business with software inside.

This is why most of the public debate gets it wrong. People argue about ten minutes versus thirty. About rider safety. About whether instant is sustainable. Those are real questions. But they sit on top of the actual question, which is: at what density does the unit-level math close.

If the density is there, the speed is a side effect. If the density isn't there, no amount of rider pressure rescues the model.

The interesting work in this category for the next three years isn't on the app. It's on a map.

A rider asked me for something nobody had thought to build — and I realised our product roadmap had been written without him in the room.

On a ride-along last month a rider asked me a question that has stayed with me.

He asked if there was a way for him to mark a building as 'climbed today' so the next rider would know which floor a tricky address was on.

It was a small ask. The kind of thing that takes a junior engineer a sprint to ship. But it was a request that had never been put on a roadmap, in eighteen years, in any product review I had been part of.

Not because we were lazy. Because the people who built the roadmap had never carried a four-storey walk-up in Powai in the rain.

For a long time I thought the gap between operator and user was the hard one to close. The rider conversation taught me there is a harder one. The gap between operator and the people who keep the operation alive on the street.

That gap is invisible from a desk. It is also the source of about half the small frictions that show up in our customer NPS without us being able to name them.

I'm not going to write a policy post about this. I'm going to do the simpler thing — keep showing up on rides, let riders ask, and put what they ask on the roadmap before any of us argue about whether it deserves to be there.

The thing he asked for is in the December release.

Most of the decisions that compound in a consumer business look small enough to skip in a board meeting.

The decisions that compound in a consumer business almost never look like the ones a board meeting will spend a slide on.

The big decisions get the slide. The small ones get the quarter.

A board slide is the right place for a category entry, an acquisition, a leadership change. But the work that actually moves a long-run number — refund speed, on-time rate at the worst hour, app load on the cheapest Android phone, the way you handle a damaged packet — that work doesn't fit on a slide.

It fits on a backlog. And the discipline is to keep funding the backlog when the board wants to talk about something more interesting.

This is the part of the job that most outside investors find boring and most inside operators find honest. The companies I've seen go from good to durable spent the last five years stubbornly making the small decisions in public, and the big decisions in private. Most of the founders who got it backwards aren't running the same companies anymore.

You can't market a backlog. You can't put a refund-speed quarter-on-quarter chart on a billboard. You can put it on the floor of a Tuesday review and stop arguing about it.

The companies that win the next decade in Indian consumer have already decided which Tuesdays matter.

The metrics consumer founders chase to prove user love are usually proxies for the wrong thing.

I think we've lost some judgment when it comes to measuring user love.

The moment we turn user love into a universally celebrated metric — NPS, app store rating, screenshot count, the number of viral tweets we got this week — we start losing sight of what actually matters, which is whether someone will open the app tomorrow when nobody is watching.

A lot of what consumer teams do with metrics makes directional sense. The missing piece is counterbalance:

1. NPS is a beautiful number. It does not predict 90-day retention in any market we operate in. We've checked.

2. App store ratings reward the customer who had a moment, not the customer who has a habit. The customer who has a habit doesn't open the rating modal because the habit is invisible to her.

3. A viral tweet about your product is a celebration of one moment, not a measure of a thousand. Shipping metrics rewarded as virality begin to look like marketing budgets disguised as product wins.

4. Adding a feature because the loudest one percent of users asked for it is a tax on the silent ninety-nine.

5. Not every problem deserves to be measured. Some are better solved than tracked.

The core idea: even if you can quantify everything, maybe you should not. The more we can measure should not reduce our need to think. It should increase it.

Watching Blinkit cross Zomato in NOV taught me that the metrics we trusted for category timing were the ones we should have questioned.

I think we lost some judgment about growth metrics in the years before Blinkit overtook Zomato in NOV.

Specifically, the metric we used to time category bets — gross order value growth — was telling us a story we wanted to hear. It said the categories we already operated were the categories that would compound. It was right about the curve. It was wrong about the slope.

Blinkit's NOV crossed Zomato's this year. Two years ago I would have said this was structurally unlikely for at least another two. The number that would have warned me was not anywhere on the slide we were reviewing. It was the slope of dark-store density per pin code in our top eight cities, which we hadn't been tracking as a primary indicator because it didn't fit our existing model of what category timing meant.

I'm not writing this to celebrate Blinkit. The team there earned that crossover with two years of work I had limited visibility into.

I'm writing this because the metric we used to predict which category would compound was the metric that fit our existing belief about which category would compound. It was a measurement of conviction, not a measurement of the market.

I haven't fully figured out what to replace it with. I know what failed. I don't yet know what passes. That's the honest answer. I'd rather sit with that than pretend I have a clean second framework already in hand.

We celebrate the hero decision; the work is the hundred small decisions that made the hero unnecessary.

I think we've lost some judgment when it comes to celebrating heroic decisions in consumer companies.

The hero decision — the bet, the pivot, the public reorganisation, the founder's clean speech at the all-hands — gets the case study, the podcast, the cover. The hundred small decisions that quietly made the heroic decision unnecessary get a footnote at best.

A lot of what consumer companies do with case studies makes directional sense. The missing piece is counterbalance:

1. The hero decision becomes the story. The boring discipline becomes the moat. We confuse the two and write postmortems about the wrong one.

2. Founders who keep getting credit for hero decisions slowly stop making the boring ones. The audience trains them out of it. The audience is the most dangerous thing about being a public founder.

3. A team that organises around hero decisions stops shipping the small wins because nobody wants to be the engineer who fixed the refund speed by twenty seconds when the press is writing about the platform pivot.

4. Investors who price companies on hero narratives systematically misprice the businesses that compound on small ones. This is not a critique of investors. It is a description of how stories travel.

5. The companies that win the next decade are mostly going to be the boring ones. They will not get the cover. They will get the customers.

The core idea: stop training your company to need a hero. The work is to make the heroic decision unnecessary.

Most planning models in Indian consumer assume the next user looks like the last one. Most won't.

Most planning models in Indian consumer companies still assume the next user looks like the last user.

They don't. The last user we acquired had a smartphone before they had savings. They had English before they had a salary. They had a friend who already used the app. None of these is true for the next two hundred million.

The next user is going to be on a phone two generations behind ours. Their data plan is going to be a third of the bandwidth. Their first language on the screen is not going to be English. Their trust threshold for a digital payment is going to be set by a single bad UPI experience their cousin had in 2023.

Most of our products are designed for the version of India that is already online. The version that is coming online is bigger and slower and more sceptical. If our app load time on a budget Android is one second slower than our competitor's, we lose them. If our refund flow needs three taps instead of two, we lose them. If our category page assumes English first, we lose them.

This is not a CSR conversation. It is a cap-table conversation. The companies that figure out how to bring the next two hundred million customers into a habit, not a one-time download, will be the durable consumer businesses of the 2030s.

The interesting work in Indian consumer for the rest of this decade is not about the user we already have. It is about the user we have not met.

When my title changed, the conversations I'd been holding for years started showing up.

In my first week not being CEO, three founders I respect called me unprompted to ask the same kind of question.

All three were questions they had wanted to ask while I was still in the chair and hadn't.

One was about a category bet they'd been wrong on for two years. One was about a co-founder dynamic that wasn't working. One was about whether they were the right person to keep running the thing they'd built.

None of them had been able to ask any of this on a board call, on Slack, in any room with witnesses.

What surprised me was not that they called. It was the volume of conversation I had been blocking from happening just by being in a public-company seat. The CEO badge does this thing where it makes other founders edit themselves around you. They want your time. They don't want your shareholder letter.

I didn't predict this. I had braced for the calendar to empty. I had not braced for the calendar to refill with conversations I'd been quietly missing for years.

I'm still working out the second-order implication. Part of me is grateful. Part of me wonders how much of the founder-to-founder honesty in this country is being held back by the seat the founder is sitting in. I don't know yet.

What I know is that the calendar I have now is harder to defend on a slide and easier to defend to myself.

A critic said my gig-work post was privilege pretending to be economics. Some of that landed.

A few weeks after I posted the long thread about how the gig economy made the labor of the poor visible to the rich, a publication wrote a piece arguing it was privilege pretending to be economics.

My first instinct was to push back. I drafted a response. I didn't send it.

Sat with the critique for a week. Some of it I disagree with. Some of it I think landed.

What landed: I wrote a 12-paragraph piece about the labor of the poor without quoting a single delivery partner in their own words. That isn't a small omission. The whole point of the piece was about visibility. I made the rider more visible to the rich and didn't make the rich more accountable to the rider. I made the writing about my own moral discovery.

That is a real critique. I don't think it invalidates the underlying observation about class invisibility. I think it shows up something I didn't do well.

The honest response to a good critique is not a counter-essay. It is a different next post. The next time I write about this, the people doing the work get to speak first and at length. I get to listen and not annotate.

This is a small commitment. It is also one of the few commitments that actually changes anything for the person whose work I claimed to make visible.

More founders kill their early company by over-hiring than by under-hiring. The mismatch is in approval cycles, not headcount.

More founders I know have killed their early companies by over-hiring than by under-hiring.

The story of under-hiring is well told. The team is too small, the founder is too thin, growth stalls because there isn't enough capacity to ship the next thing. That story shows up in podcasts, in recruiter pitches, in board memos.

The story of over-hiring shows up nowhere because it doesn't have a clean villain. The team got bigger. The cap table got crowded. Decision speed dropped. The thing that used to take a Slack message now takes a meeting. The thing that used to take a meeting now takes a process. The thing that used to take a process now takes a quarter.

By the time the founder notices, the company has slowed by half and nobody can point to the moment.

The mismatch is not in headcount. It is in approval cycles. A team that doubles in size needs more than twice the discipline to keep its decisions fast. Most founders don't budget for that discipline because the discipline doesn't show up in any forecasting tool.

My rough rule, having watched this in maybe forty consumer companies in India, including my own at different stages: hire a third slower than your fundraise lets you. Spend the surplus on better people in fewer seats. The pain of being slightly under-hired is recoverable. The pain of being over-hired is structural.

The companies that look thin from the outside are usually the ones moving fastest from the inside.
