rows.col_3: Rider density observation
rows.col_4: A pattern in our rider attrition data didn't match the model we'd been operating on for two years.
rows.col_5:
A pattern in our rider attrition data this year has been quietly bothering me.

The riders who stay longest in our network are not the ones in the densest catchments where the trip-per-hour math is best. They are the ones in slightly less dense catchments where the routes are longer and the trip-per-hour is lower.

This is the opposite of what our operating model predicts.

For two years our operations team has optimised dispatch for trip-per-hour, on the theory that rider income drives rider retention. The theory is right at the population level. It's wrong at the cohort level.

The rider conversations I've sat in on this quarter suggest the people staying longest aren't optimising for income alone. They are optimising for predictability. A slightly lower hourly that comes from a stable route they know is worth more to them than a higher hourly that comes from a chaotic dispatch in a too-dense pin code.

I don't yet know what to do with this. The operating model that drove our rider retention strategy for two years may be partly wrong. Or it may be right at scale and wrong in the cohorts we should care about most. Both are possible.

What I am sure of is that the next version of the model has to start by talking to the riders who have been on the platform the longest, and asking them what predictability looks like to them.

That conversation has been overdue.

rows.col_3: Pundits drowning out operators
rows.col_4: The AI-in-consumer commentary has become a second job for people who haven't shipped, and operators are paying the cost.
rows.col_5:
It's getting weird out here.

Everyone and their cousin has a thesis on AI in consumer now. Some guy discovered LLM-driven recommendation three weeks ago and is publishing a 6,000-word manifesto. Someone with no operating experience and a Substack is telling Indian quick-commerce founders they're 'missing the agent moment.' A McKinsey associate just dropped a deck full of nine boxes about how AI will eat retail.

Nobody knows what is actually going on. Not you. Not me. Not the consultant with eighty thousand followers cranking out hot takes like it's a second job. Which, to be fair, it probably is.

Some weeks AI saves my product team three days of work. Some weeks they lose four arguing with a model that confidently gets the GST math wrong on a refund flow.

The only honest advice I have for consumer founders right now: we have been here before. Print to digital. SMS to push. Web to mobile. App store to super app. Every time the platform shifted, somebody on a stage said the old playbook was dead and somebody else just kept shipping the version of their product that the customer wanted.

Try things. Break them. Refund the customers your AI summary got wrong. Ship the boring fix. Try again. Touch grass. Turn the agent off when it stops earning its keep. The only framework that has ever worked in Indian consumer is to keep the customer in the room when the rest of the world is in a green room.

That's the framework. That has always been the framework.

rows.col_3: 10-min commentary chaos
rows.col_4: The public conversation about quick commerce is louder than the people actually running quick commerce — and the gap shows in the writing.
rows.col_5:
It's getting weird out here in the quick-commerce commentary lane.

Everyone has a hot take on dark stores now. Someone who has never approved a lease just discovered catchment math and posted a thread. A media commentator wrote a 2,000-word piece on rider safety using zero conversations with actual riders. A Twitter account with 200,000 followers and no operating context is telling founders the model is structurally broken.

Nobody talking about this loudly is operating it. Not the columnist. Not the consultant. Not the analyst who has a price target on the parent company.

Some days I want to write a long reply. I don't. The thing about commentary is that it travels faster than evidence. Replying gives the bad take more air than ignoring it does.

The only sentence I have for this category right now: most public takes about dark stores are wrong because the math has to close at the pin-code level, and almost nobody outside the operating teams has the pin-code-level data.

You can argue with the takes. You can also keep adding stores in pin codes where the catchment closes and not adding them in pin codes where it doesn't. The second job is harder. It is also the only one that builds a category.

The commentary will sort itself out. The math doesn't have to wait for the commentary to be right.

rows.col_3: He himself contributed to the chaos
rows.col_4: I've been one of the loud voices in this conversation. Stepping back made me realise how much of my own writing was contributing to the chaos I now find exhausting.
rows.col_5:
It's getting weird out here. I've been part of why.

For most of the last three years I've published thoughts on quick commerce, AI, gig work, and category strategy with the volume and authority of someone running the largest player in the category. I was. Some of those posts were good. Some of them were the kind of thing I now find exhausting to read from other people.

I noticed it about a month after stepping back as CEO. I sat down to write a post about a Q4 trend and caught myself pre-loading the same kind of confident, capital-T-Take energy that I used to roll my eyes at when other people did it. The take might have been right. The energy was the problem.

Loud confidence works on this platform. It also makes the conversation worse for the people actually trying to operate.

What I've been trying to do since: write less when I'm certain and more when I'm uncertain. Publish the wrestle, not the conclusion. Resist the urge to land every post on a clean line that gets screenshotted.

It's harder than it sounds. The screenshot is what travels. The wrestle is what's true.

I'm not sure how this lands. I know which one I'd rather be writing.

rows.col_3: Habit beats love
rows.col_4: Consumer-product retention compounds on habit — not on the moments your customer brags about.
rows.col_5:
Most consumer founders I talk to right now are optimising for the moments their customers brag about.

They are paying for the wrong thing.

The customer who tweets about your product once is not the customer who keeps your business alive. The customer who keeps your business alive is the one who opens the app at 9:43 p.m. on a Wednesday in October without thinking about it. The screenshot customer comes back when the wallet flow you ran has a discount. The autopilot customer comes back because she does not remember a time she did not.

You build the autopilot customer one quarter at a time. The wallet flow does not build her. The marketing campaign does not build her. The 'delight moment' does not build her. The boring choice to refund without arguing builds her. The decision not to add a fee in a tight quarter builds her. The discipline to not change the home screen because growth has a thesis builds her.

Most of the consumer companies I have watched in India for the last decade have wanted the screenshot customer. The ones still standing built the autopilot customer.

Habit is not glamorous. It is what you have when the marketing budget runs out and the user still opens the app.

That is the moat.

rows.col_3: Class invisibility / rider observation
rows.col_4: A rider asked me a question that exposed a gap in our product process I'd been blind to for years.
rows.col_5:
On a recent ride-along a rider asked me why our app, which lets him see his own earnings to the rupee, didn't let his wife see them.

He explained: when his earnings dropped one week, his wife thought he had been spending money on something he wasn't. The opacity to her was the source of an argument they had at home that lasted three days.

It was not a feature request. It was a description of a friction in his life that our product was creating without anyone on our team knowing.

I have sat in product reviews for eighteen years. We have built family-share features for customers. We had not built them for riders. The reason was not technical. The reason was that the rider had not been imagined as a person who has a household to be transparent with.

That is what class invisibility looks like inside a product backlog. Not malice. A failure of imagination at the moment somebody types up the ticket.

I'm not going to write a policy post about this. The simpler version is this: in the next four product reviews I sit in on, the first question I am going to ask is not what this builds for the customer. It is what this builds for, or against, the rider's household.

The thing he asked for is on the December backlog. The bigger thing — the question we should have been asking on every backlog — is what I'm trying to fix.

rows.col_3: Next-billion blindspot
rows.col_4: Indian consumer planning models are still designed around the user we already have, not the next 200M.
rows.col_5:
India is going to add roughly two hundred million internet users in the next four years. Almost none of our consumer apps are designed for them.

The user we already have was acquired in a window that is closing. She had a smartphone before she had savings. She had English before she had a salary. She had a friend who had used the app before she did. Almost none of these is true for the user coming next.

The next user is on a phone two generations behind ours. Her data plan is one-third the bandwidth. Her first language on the screen is not English. Her trust threshold for digital payments was set by a single bad UPI experience her cousin had three years ago.

Our apps assume otherwise. The category page leads with English. The refund flow needs three taps. The video previews assume bandwidth we no longer have. The customer-support handover assumes a chat language we don't write in.

None of these is hard to fix. They are unattended because the user we are designing for is already in the funnel. The user we are about to meet has not entered it.

The companies that compound in Indian consumer for the rest of this decade will be the ones who treat the next two hundred million as the design target, not the upgrade lane.

The growth is not in the user we already have. It is in the user we have not introduced our app to.

rows.col_3: Earnings-call honesty
rows.col_4: The most useful answer I gave on the Q3 call was the one I almost didn't.
rows.col_5:
The most useful answer I gave on the Q3 earnings call was the one I almost didn't.

An analyst asked about a slowdown in one of our smaller categories. The standard answer was ready: tough comparison base, short-term seasonality, structural growth intact. I had said versions of it before.

What came out instead was: we made the wrong call on inventory in this category two quarters ago and we're still working through the consequences. It will take us another two quarters to clear it.

The room went quieter than usual. Two analysts asked follow-up questions. One sent a separate note afterwards saying it was the most useful exchange they had had on a quarterly call in a year.

I have spent eighteen years preparing for earnings calls. I have only recently figured out that the most valuable thing a CEO can do on one is to occasionally not perform.

The pressure to perform on these calls is enormous and not always honest. The metric the analyst is evaluating you on is whether your story holds together. The metric the long-term shareholder is evaluating you on is whether your judgement is reliable. The two are not always the same.

I am still learning when to push back on the polished answer the team has prepared for me. I get it wrong more often than I'd like.

The exchange that quarter was a small thing. It also taught me something I was overdue learning.

rows.col_3: Boring discipline beats heroic decisions
rows.col_4: The unsexy weekly review is where a consumer company is actually built — not the strategy offsite.
rows.col_5:
In every consumer company I have been close to, the moment that decides whether the business compounds is not the strategy offsite. It is the Tuesday review.

The strategy offsite gets the budget, the photographer, the deck. It gets the news cycle. The Tuesday review gets a meeting room with bad lighting and a backlog the founders are sometimes too tired to argue about.

The Tuesday review is where the boring decisions happen. The refund-cycle is dropping by twelve seconds — should we ship the new flow or wait. The drop-off rate on the third onboarding screen is up 1.4 percentage points in tier-two cities — what changed. Two pin codes have a courier integration timeout that is causing a quiet 0.6 percent of orders to fail silently — fix this week or next.

None of those decisions is exciting. None of them survives a slide deck. All of them compound.

The consumer companies that look durable five years in are the ones whose Tuesday reviews became sharper, faster, and more honest, year over year. The ones that look fragile are the ones where the offsite got better and the Tuesday review got skipped.

You can't market a Tuesday review. You can't put one in an investor deck. You can put a calendar invite for it on the operating team's week and not move it.

That, in my experience, is the difference.

rows.col_3: Open-source thinking pre-peer-review
rows.col_4: I keep choosing to publish unfinished thinking publicly because peer feedback in the wild outpaces what I'd get in a closed loop.
rows.col_5:
I keep choosing to publish unfinished thinking on this platform and I am still not sure whether that is the right call.

The argument for it is that the disagreement that arrives in the comments is often sharper than what I would have generated alone. The hypothesis I published in November is sharper today because three neuroscientists, two physicists, and a pulmonologist took the time to publicly disagree with parts of it.

The argument against it is that the unfinished thinking gets read as finished thinking by people who don't follow closely. The hypothesis I marked clearly as 'open question' got headlines that read as 'Goyal claims.' That is a real cost.

I have not figured out how to net these two against each other. I keep choosing to publish for now because the peer-review loop in academia takes 18 to 24 months and I am 47. The math on closed-loop iteration does not work for someone trying to do real work in their remaining decades of cognitive bandwidth.

This is the calculation I'm making. I don't recommend it for everyone. It's possible that I am partly wrong about the trade. It's also possible that the cost of being wrong in public is what eventually makes the work better.

What I'm sure of is that more good ideas have died waiting for the perfect moment to publish than have died from being published too early.

That conviction is doing most of the work for me right now.

rows.col_3: Operating distance is not an edge
rows.col_4: The 'I'm not from this industry, that's my edge' line is one of the most expensive myths in Indian consumer.
rows.col_5:
'I am not from this industry — that's my edge.' I have heard this line in maybe a hundred consumer pitches in the last decade. It is one of the most expensive myths in Indian consumer.

The more you understand the operations, the better your decisions, the sharper your read on what to subsidise and what to leave alone, the faster you catch the bullshit a vendor is selling you, the less likely you are to spend a year solving the wrong problem.

What changes is which kinds of operating depth matter. The intricacies of a particular ERP, the finer details of one warehouse layout, the exact configuration of a delivery routing engine — those don't matter as much to everyone in a leadership seat. The pieces fit together differently than they did ten years ago.

But you should absolutely understand how the operating system of your category works. From customer to rider to dark store to vendor to landlord. Learn how the basket gets composed because you'll spot the SKUs that are killing you. Learn how the dispatch logic decides who gets which order because you'll catch the pattern when riders quit. Learn how the refund flow works because that flow is the back door to your retention number. Learn what shrinkage looks like in a tier-2 store because if you don't, somebody else's career will be built on it.

Distance from the work is not an edge. It is a debt the operators on your team are quietly paying for you.

rows.col_3: Founders should write in public
rows.col_4: The 'don't write in public, it's a distraction' founder advice is wrong — writing in public is the cheapest thinking tool a founder has.
rows.col_5:
'Don't write in public, it's a distraction.' I have heard this from investors more times than I can count. It is wrong.

The more publicly you think, the faster you find out which of your ideas are weaker than you thought, the sooner you correct, the less private capital you spend defending positions you should have changed.

What changes is which writing helps. The performative thread, the take with no skin in it, the manufactured controversy — those don't help anyone. Most of what passes for founder writing on this platform is broadcast, not thinking.

But you should absolutely write the half-formed observations you are genuinely sitting with. Learn to publish the question, not the conclusion. Learn to write about the thing you got wrong last quarter, because the comments will tell you whether you got it less wrong than you thought or more wrong than you admitted. Learn to write about the small moments that don't fit the slide deck — the rider conversation, the analyst question, the customer complaint — because those are the ones the data later tells you mattered most.

Most founders I know who don't write in public say it's because they are too busy. The honest version is that they are too unsure. Writing in public is uncomfortable. The discomfort is the point.

You don't write in public to grow followers. You write in public to find out what you actually think.

rows.col_3: CEO seat is not the safest place to think
rows.col_4: The CEO chair felt like the safest place to think in public. It was actually the most constrained.
rows.col_5:
'The CEO chair is the best vantage point a founder can have.' I told myself this for at least four years. I was wrong.

The CEO chair is a great vantage point for running the operation. It is a constrained one for thinking out loud about the operation.

For most of my time as Group CEO, I felt like the public seat gave me the right perspective and the institutional protection to think about hard things in public. Quarterly results, public-company governance, audit oversight — all of it should add up to a clearer view, right?

What it actually did was edit me. Each draft I wrote about a category I was still working through, a hire I had gotten wrong, a number I didn't fully understand — got reshaped on the way out. Some of the editing was prudent. A lot of it was self-protection masquerading as prudence.

I noticed it most in the first three weeks after I stepped away. The same drafts felt different. The thinking I had been compressing into press-release shapes started decompressing into actual sentences.

What I am still working out: how much of that constraint was the seat, and how much was me. I want to believe it was mostly the seat. I suspect it was mostly me, and the seat was just convenient cover.

This is not a complaint about the CEO role. It is a description of a cost I had not fully named to myself before.

rows.col_3: QC is real estate
rows.col_4: The interesting work in quick commerce is in lease negotiations, not in app updates.
rows.col_5:
The most interesting hire we made in quick commerce in the last two years was not on the engineering team. It was a real-estate sourcing lead who had spent ten years inside a large-format retail chain.

This is the part of the category most people miss when they describe it as a tech business. The thing that decides whether you make money in any given pin code is not the dispatch algorithm. It is whether your sourcing team got a 1,800-square-foot box at the right rent within 1.6 kilometres of the right households.

The dispatch algorithm matters. The app matters. The catalogue matters. None of them matter as much as the lease, the layout, and the time it takes to get a power approval and a fire NOC.

This is why the operating teams in this category, in India, look much more like retail teams than tech teams. The headcount mix is heavier on operations, lighter on product. The board meetings are heavier on real estate updates, lighter on roadmap reviews. The week-on-week metric that decides whether the category compounds is store productivity by catchment, not feature velocity by sprint.

Most of the public conversation gets this wrong because it imports the SaaS model of how a business gets built. SaaS businesses get built in code. This one gets built in lease negotiations.

The interesting work in this category for the next three years is on the map, not on the app.

rows.col_3: Habit insight from Blinkit crossover
rows.col_4: The reason Blinkit's NOV crossed Zomato's was that the habit form factor changed faster than I'd expected.
rows.col_5:
Blinkit's NOV crossed Zomato's NOV this year. I did not predict it would happen this fast.

Two years ago I would have said the food-delivery habit was older, deeper, and more defensible than the quick-commerce habit. The frequency was lower, the basket smaller, the customer retention shape less consistent.

What I missed was that habit in consumer is a function of trip frequency, not trip emotion. The customer who orders groceries three times a week builds a stronger autopilot than the customer who orders dinner twice. The food-delivery habit lives in the dinner moment. The quick-commerce habit lives across the day, and the day has more moments than dinner does.

This is obvious in hindsight. It was not obvious to me eighteen months ago.

What I'm sitting with now: the habit form factor in Indian consumer is shifting faster than the planning models I built three years ago can keep up with. The category that compounds fastest is not necessarily the one with the best margins, the deepest moat, or the most loved brand. It is the one whose use case maps to the most moments in a customer's week.

This has implications I have not fully worked through. The category I would build today is not the one I would have built in 2022. I'm spending most of my reading time right now on what frequency-of-use means in tier-2 cities, where the moments map differently than in metros.

I don't have a clean conclusion. I have a sharper version of the question than I had two years ago.

rows.col_3: Over-staffing kills
rows.col_4: You can't add headcount and add discipline at the same rate — and most founders quietly add discipline last.
rows.col_5:
You can't add headcount and add decision discipline at the same rate. Most founders quietly add discipline last, and that is where the company starts dying.

Headcount is the easy variable. You raise a round. You hire. The team grows. The slide deck looks healthier.

Decision discipline is the hard variable. The thing that used to take a Slack message now takes a meeting. The thing that used to take a meeting now takes a process. The thing that used to take a process now takes a quarter. Each step adds clarity. Each step adds days.

Most founders see the days. Few see the compounding. By the time the founders notice that the company is six weeks slower per decision than it was a year ago, the operating habit is hard to reset.

The fix is not to hire less in absolute terms. It is to budget the discipline-cost of every new seat at the time the seat is created. A new VP is not just a comp line. It is a process line, an alignment line, a reporting line, a communication line. Most companies model the comp. Few model the friction.

The companies that look thin from the outside are usually the ones that did this math and made the harder choice in fewer seats.

In Indian consumer, where decision speed is one of the few real moats against larger global comps, this is not a small thing. It is the thing.

rows.col_3: Newslaundry critique landed
rows.col_4: A critic accused me of writing about gig workers without quoting them. Most of that landed.
rows.col_5:
A few weeks after my long thread about how gig work made the labor of the poor visible, a publication wrote that the piece was 'privilege pretending to be economics.'

My first instinct was a counter-essay. I drafted it. I deleted it.

Sat with the critique for a week. Then a second week.

What I think the critic got right: I wrote 12 paragraphs about the lives of riders without quoting a single rider in their own language. I made the rider visible to the rich. I did not make the rich more accountable to the rider. The piece was about my moral discovery, not about the rider's economic situation.

That distinction is not a small one. The whole point of the original thread was visibility. By making myself the protagonist of a piece about visibility, I narrowed the visibility I claimed to be expanding.

What I think the critic got partly wrong: the underlying observation about class invisibility being a structural feature of pre-gig capitalism is not undone by my failure to centre the rider's voice. The argument can be right and the writer can be a poor witness for it.

I am still working through what to do about this. The next time I write about this, the people doing the work get to speak first. I get to listen, not annotate. That is a small commitment. It is also one of the few that actually changes anything for the person whose work I claimed to make visible.

This post is not a defence. It is the credit I should have given when the critique landed.

rows.col_3: Biology + compute
rows.col_4: The next decade's defining founders will be working at the intersection of biology and compute, not in either alone.
rows.col_5:
The defining founder of the 2030s will not be working in pure software or pure biology. They will be working in the place where the two collide.

For the last twenty years compute has been ahead of biology by a wide margin. Software ate everything because software could be measured, iterated, and shipped on a weekly cadence. Biology could be measured but iterated and shipped on a decade cadence. The mismatch made software the more attractive place to put a career.

That gap is closing. Cheap genomic sequencing closed part of it. Wearables closed part of it. Foundation models trained on protein structures closed a lot of it. The cadence at which a biology hypothesis can be tested is now measured in weeks for several classes of question, not decades.

This is what makes the next decade different from the last one. The interesting compounding work will be at the seams. Cardiology and continuous monitoring. Cognition and wearable EEG. Aging biology and personal blood-flow data. Reproductive biology and at-home diagnostics.

None of these is a pure software bet. None of these is a pure biology bet. All of them require a founder who can sit at the intersection long enough to be wrong publicly several times before getting one thing right.

That is most of why I am putting my own time and capital into Continue Research. The category I want to spend my remaining decades in is the one where the cadence of biology has caught up to the cadence of software.

That category is not yet crowded. It will be.

rows.col_3: First week post-CEO honesty
rows.col_4: What surprised me most about the first week not being CEO was the silence I had been mistaking for clarity.
rows.col_5:
The first week I was not CEO of Eternal, the thing that surprised me was the volume of background noise that disappeared.

For eighteen years I had a chronic mid-volume hum — the inbox, the calendar, the half-tracked decisions, the four open Slacks, the analyst note that was almost certainly going to need a response, the regulatory item, the comp committee email, the press inquiry, the small operational decision I had told someone I would weigh in on by Tuesday.

All of it left at the same time. The calendar didn't just empty. The hum stopped.

What unsettled me was that I had been using the hum as a proxy for clarity. If the hum was loud, I was on top of things. If the hum was quiet, I was missing something. I had been measuring my own competence by the volume of noise around me.

The first week of quiet was uncomfortable in a way I had not predicted. I caught myself opening Slack for no reason. I caught myself drafting emails I had no business drafting. I felt, briefly, useless.

By the end of the second week, the quiet started feeling like room. By the end of the fourth, it started feeling like a tool.

I don't yet know what the right next thing to do with the quiet is. I know that for the first month I had been confusing being needed with being useful. Those are not the same thing.

Still working it out.

rows.col_3: Scale of next-billion blindspot
rows.col_4: Most Indian consumer apps are designed for the 8% already in the funnel — the other 92% will define the next decade.
rows.col_5:
Picture a grid where each dot is 14 million Indians. The whole grid is 1.4 billion people.

About 700 million have a smartphone today. About 110 million transact regularly on a quick-commerce or food-delivery app. Roughly 8 million pay for any consumer subscription priced above ₹500/month.

If you build for Indian consumer, your customer pool today is the 8 million in that last band. Your future is in the 110 million in the second band, the 700 million in the first band, and the 700 million who don't yet have a phone.

Most of our planning models, including some of mine over the years, assumed the next user looked like the last one. Almost none of them will. The next user is two phone generations behind, on a third the bandwidth, in a language that is not English, with a trust threshold for a digital payment that was set by a single bad UPI experience her cousin had three years ago.

We are not at the end of Indian consumer adoption. We are not even at the beginning of the middle. The category is still being introduced to most of the country.

For founders, this means the market you are addressing today is a fraction of where it is going. For investors, the companies that figure out how to move from the top 8 million to the next 100 million will define the next decade. For operators, the only honest unit of planning right now is the customer who has not yet entered your funnel.

The conversation on this app is saturated. The actual market is wide open.

rows.col_3: Scale of the price gap
rows.col_4: The price gap between Indian and US consumer is one of the most under-discussed structural facts in our market.
rows.col_5:
A typical Indian consumer pays roughly one-sixth of what a typical US consumer pays for the same end product.

Think about that. Same SKU. Same packaging requirements at the spec level. Same expectation of freshness, freight, and refund. One-sixth the willingness to pay.

This is not because the Indian consumer is cheap. It is because the Indian consumer's median household income is roughly one-tenth of the US consumer's, and her share-of-wallet for any non-essential category is structurally smaller.

What this means for the operator is that everything in the supply chain has to be one-sixth as expensive to deliver. Not slightly cheaper. Structurally cheaper. The packaging is thinner. The fulfilment is denser. The marketing channels are more efficient. The headcount mix is heavier on operations and lighter on the parts of the org chart that look glamorous on a slide.

Most international funds plug Indian companies into the same financial models they use for their US comps. Margins look 'thin.' EBITDA looks 'depressed.' Take rates look 'underdeveloped.' The model is wrong. Indian consumer businesses are running on a different gravity, not a different version of the same gravity.

For Indian founders: stop apologising for the gravity. The gravity is the design constraint. The companies that figure out how to compound at this gravity become uniquely durable in a market the rest of the world cannot easily enter.

For international investors: build the Indian financial model from scratch. The US one will give you the wrong answer.

rows.col_3: Scale of class invisibility
rows.col_4: For most of capitalism the labor of the poor was structurally invisible to the rich. The gig economy collapsed that — and we still don't know how to handle it.
rows.col_5:
For most of recorded capitalism, the labor of the poor has been physically invisible to the rich. Factory workers were behind walls. Farmers were in distant fields. Domestic workers were in back rooms.

The gig economy collapsed that. The person delivering your groceries is on your screen with a name, a face, an ETA, and a star rating. The labor is no longer behind a wall. The wall has been removed.

This is the structural feature of the last decade that gets the least serious attention. We are the first generation in human history to consume the labor of the poor with the door open.

I wrote a long thread about this earlier this year. I got it partly wrong. A critic pointed out that I made the rider visible to the rich without making the rich more accountable to the rider. The critique landed. I have been thinking about it for weeks.

What I have not figured out: the visibility is real. The accountability is not yet structural. We have invented a labor relationship in which the customer can see the worker but the worker has no clean recourse to the customer. That asymmetry is not what visibility was supposed to produce.

I am still working out what it would mean for visibility to translate into something other than guilt at the door. The platform has a role here that we have not yet earned the right to claim.

This is the work of the next phase. I do not know how it ends.

rows.col_3: Structural vs subsidised QC
rows.col_4: Most of the QC business in India is one of two models stacked on top of each other — and the customer can't tell which.
rows.col_5:
Most of the quick-commerce in India today is one of two business models running under the same brand.

Model A: dense catchment, rich basket, structural margin. The dark store covers a 1.6 km radius with enough households at the right AOV that the unit economics close at the speed offered. The rider is moving fast because the system is designed for fast.

Model B: thinner catchment, subsidised speed. The dark store is in a pin code where the basket is too small or the density is too low to make the math close at the promised SLA. The speed is being paid for by the operating P&L, the rider's effective hourly, or somebody's investor patience.

From the customer's screen the two models look identical. Same app. Same ten minutes. Same packet. The model only diverges in the unit math the operator has access to.

Most of the public conversation about quick commerce conflates the two. It treats the speed as the same number across the network. It is not. A 1,200-store network running half on Model A and half on Model B looks like one business and is structurally two.

The operators who survive the next two years in this category are the ones who are already converting Model B stores to Model A — by adding category depth, by raising AOV, by closing the stores where the math will not bend.

That work isn't visible in the app. It is the only work that matters.

rows.col_3: My writing got more declarative
rows.col_4: My LinkedIn writing has gotten more polished and less honest in the same year.
rows.col_5:
I went back through my LinkedIn drafts folder this weekend.

What I noticed: the posts I shipped in the last six months are sharper, cleaner, and more declarative than the ones I shipped in 2023 and 2024. They land harder. The screenshot rate is higher.

They are also less honest. Most of them are answers I had already arrived at before I sat down to write. I was performing arrival, not arriving.

The posts in 2023 that I am most proud of are the ones where I was working something out in the act of writing. They were less polished. They got fewer reposts. They were also where most of my actual thinking happened that year.

This is the trade founders make on this platform without quite naming it. The platform rewards the polished take. The thinking happens in the wrestling. You can choose either, but you can't choose both at the same time.

For most of the last twelve months I had been quietly choosing the screenshot. Stepping back as CEO has made me notice that. I am trying to course-correct. The next ten posts you see from me are likely to be less polished and more honest. I expect engagement to drop. That is mostly the point.

I am still working out where the right balance sits. I am not yet sure. What I am sure of is that the version of my writing that has landed well lately is not the version that has helped me think.

rows.col_3: Profit/growth restraint
rows.col_4: The 5.3% margin is the residue of the categories we chose not to fund.
rows.col_5:
Our food-delivery business hit a 5.3 percent margin on net order value last quarter. It is the highest margin we have ever posted in this category.

The margin did not come from a pricing increase. It did not come from a vendor renegotiation. It did not come from a single feature launch.

It came from the categories we chose not to fund.

For the last four quarters we have been more disciplined than we historically were about which expansion bets we put real capital behind. Most of the categories we passed on were ones with a clean-looking GMV story and an unclean unit-math story. The clean GMV story made the category attractive on a slide. The unclean unit math made the long-run compounding fragile.

In the previous version of our operating model we would have funded several of those bets anyway, on the theory that we could fix the unit math at scale. We were wrong about that theory in two specific cases earlier. We learned. We applied the lesson.

The margin number is the residue of the no's. It is not a product of cleverness. It is a product of restraint repeated long enough to compound.

Most consumer founders treat profit and growth as a slider. They are not. They are two outcomes of the same input — which growth you choose to fund, and which growth you decide is just expensive marketing dressed in a better word.

rows.col_3: Temple data point
rows.col_4: The most useful insight from a year of Temple data wasn't the one I was tracking — it was the one that surprised me.
rows.col_5:
After a year of wearing Temple, the data point I keep coming back to is one I was not tracking at the start.

I was looking at the relationship between time-on-feet and cerebral blood flow. Posture was the variable I had built the hypothesis around.

What kept showing up in the noise was something else. The largest day-over-day swings in cerebral blood flow happened in the first thirty minutes of focused cognitive work. Specifically, when I sat down to write something hard.

This is one observation, on one body, on a non-clinical instrument. It is not a hypothesis. It is barely an anecdote.

But it has done something to how I think about the next phase of Continue Research's work. The original hypothesis treated cerebral blood flow as primarily a posture phenomenon. The data is hinting that posture is one input and cognition is another, and that the relative weight of the two is not what I assumed.

What I'm learning to sit with: the data you collect with the lens you brought is not necessarily the data that turns out to matter. Most of the interesting science I have read in the last year started with somebody noticing something they were not looking for and following it.

That is the discipline I am trying to learn. Not just to test the hypothesis I started with — but to look at the noise around it and ask what it might be a signal of.

I do not yet know where this leads.

rows.col_3: Hire for distance from comfort
rows.col_4: Distance-from-comfort is more predictive of early hire performance than any credential filter.
rows.col_5:
The single best filter I have used for early hires in the last decade is the candidate's distance from comfort.

This is not a sentimental filter. It is a practical one.

In an early-stage Indian consumer company, the work is full of weeks where the answer to the next question is not in any deck and not in any podcast. The candidate has to figure out how to get a kitchen partner approval in a tier-3 city where neither party speaks the other's first language. They have to figure out why a refund flow is silently failing for one cohort in Lucknow but not in Pune. They have to negotiate a rider parking arrangement at a mall at 11 p.m. on a Sunday.

None of that work is taught at any school. The credential gap between a top-tier graduate and a small-town engineer who built her own way out of two unrelated industries is, in my experience of about a hundred consumer hires across companies I have backed or built, not predictive of who solves these problems faster.

Distance from comfort is. Candidates who have moved through real friction in their own life have a calibration I cannot teach in any onboarding. They know what is recoverable and what isn't. They don't panic at the third unknown.

The credentialed candidate is often easier to hire. They are also often the wrong person for the work that defines whether the company survives its first three years.

Hire the scar.

rows.col_3: Boring-margin analyst answer
rows.col_4: What I should have said to the analyst on the Q3 call: there is no moment.
rows.col_5:
On the Q3 earnings call last quarter an analyst asked me a question I gave the standard answer to and have been thinking about ever since.

He asked which decision drove the 5.3 percent margin on food delivery — the highest in our history. I gave him the rehearsed reply. Category maturity. Operating leverage. Disciplined capital allocation. The line moved on.

What I should have said: there is no decision. There is no moment. The margin is the residue of about a hundred small no's spread over six quarters. None of those no's was heroic. Most of them I forgot inside a week. They added up the way compound interest adds up — invisibly, until somebody asks you on a call to point at the moment.

The reason I gave the standard answer was that the standard answer is what the call is built for. Earnings calls reward stories that compress cleanly into a quote. They penalise the truer answer that does not.

This is a small failure. It is also one I am noticing more often. The structures we build for communicating with capital have a shape, and the shape is incompatible with the messy way that durable businesses actually get built.

I'm trying to work out what to do with this. Maybe write the longer answer here, on this platform, where the structure is more forgiving. Maybe push back on the rehearsed answer in the room next time.

I haven't decided. I'm sitting with it.

rows.col_3: QC hype cycle vs operating reality
rows.col_4: The category's hype shifted every year; the underlying problem stayed the same.
rows.col_5:
2022 hype: 'Quick commerce will replace kirana.'
2023 hype: 'Quick commerce will replace large-format retail.'
2024 hype: '10-minute delivery is the future of all of e-commerce.'
2025 hype: 'Dark stores are uneconomical at scale.'
2026 hype: 'Quick commerce is the only e-commerce model that matters in India.'

Reality: we're still figuring out how to get the unit math to close in tier-2 city pin codes where the basket size is half of what we see in metros and the catchment density is a third.

The hype cycle around this category has shifted every year for five straight years. The underlying operating problem has not changed. It is, and was, and will continue to be: at what pin-code density does the dark-store math close.

Most of the public conversation rotates around whichever framing is fashionable that quarter. The operating teams in the category mostly ignore the framing and keep working on the same set of unsexy questions. Catchment maps. Lease renewals. Refund-cycle compression. SKU rationalisation. Rider attrition.

None of those questions makes a thread. All of them decide whether the business is here in five years.

The hype cycle is a mirror. The work is the work.

rows.col_3: Consumer-AI hype vs habit
rows.col_4: Every year someone names what AI will replace in consumer; the answer is always 'nothing the customer cares about yet.'
rows.col_5:
2023 hype: 'AI will replace your search bar.'
2024 hype: 'AI will replace your category page.'
2025 hype: 'AI will replace your customer support team.'
2026 hype: 'AI will be your storefront.'

Reality: most consumer apps in India still see less than 4 percent of customer sessions touch any LLM-driven surface, and the customer who touches one once does not necessarily come back to it.

The hype around AI in consumer has been louder than the customer behaviour for four years. Some of that gap is timing — the next billion users are not online yet, and the customer who is online has habits that don't bend on quarterly product timelines.

Most of the gap is something else. AI in consumer has been chasing the cool demo, not the boring habit. The cool demo lives on a podcast. The boring habit lives at 9:43 p.m. on a Wednesday in October when the customer opens the app on autopilot.

The interesting AI work in consumer for the next two years is not in the agentic storefront. It is in the silent backend — the recommendation that doesn't surprise the customer, the refund that processes without a human in the loop, the retention model that catches the cohort before it churns.

The hype loves the front of the house. The compounding lives in the back of the house.

rows.col_3: Founder-transition hype vs his actual reality
rows.col_4: Every year of my tenure I'd told myself a story about what stepping back would feel like. None of them survived contact with the actual week.
rows.col_5:
2019 me: 'When I step back, I'll spend more time with the team.'
2021 me: 'When I step back, I'll finally have time to read.'
2023 me: 'When I step back, I'll start the next thing immediately.'
2025 me: 'When I step back, I'll finally figure out what I actually think.'

2026 reality: I have been not-CEO for two months and I have done none of the four things on that list.

What I have done instead is sit in an unusual amount of quiet, drafted and deleted a lot of half-finished writing, opened Slack out of habit and closed it without responding, and slowly started noticing how much of my self-image had been built around the specific weight of the previous job.

None of the founder-transition stories I had told myself prepared me for the actual texture of the first eight weeks. The narratives I had built — productive sabbatical, second-act energy, monastic clarity — were marketing copy I had written for myself.

What's actually happening is harder to summarise. The clarity is not arriving on the schedule I expected. The energy is not the kind I had budgeted for. The reading list is mostly untouched. The new thing has a shape but not a sentence.

I don't have a clean update yet. What I have learned is that founder-transition hype is roughly as accurate as any other hype cycle.

Mostly: not very.

rows.col_3: Global standards / local prices
rows.col_4: Indian consumer founders are running a structurally different game than their US comp — and the financial models keep mispricing both.
rows.col_5:
Same product. One-sixth the price. Faster delivery than the comp gets in San Francisco. That is the design constraint of Indian consumer.

It is not a bug in the model. It is the gravity of the country. Median household income here is roughly one-tenth of US median household income. The customer's willingness to pay for any non-essential category is structurally smaller. Every consumer business in the country either learns to build for it or stops being a consumer business in the country.

What it looks like to build for it: strip cost everywhere the customer can't see. Keep cost everywhere she can. Run on margins that the international comp considers structurally impossible. Compound them slowly with a discipline that the international comp considers boring.

International funds keep applying the US model and asking why our margins look depressed. They don't. They look depressed because the model is wrong, not because the business is.

For Indian operators: stop apologising for the gravity. Build for it. The companies that get this gravity are uniquely durable in a market that the rest of the world has not figured out how to operate in.

The gravity is not a complaint. It is the moat.

rows.col_3: Rider density observation
rows.col_4: What riders optimise for at the cohort level isn't what we'd assumed at the population level.
rows.col_5:
A pattern in our rider attrition data over the last four quarters has been bothering me.

At the population level, our model is right: higher trips-per-hour correlates with lower rider attrition. The dispatch logic is built around that.

At the cohort level — riders who have been with us for more than 18 months — the pattern reverses. The ones who stay longest are not in the densest catchments where trips-per-hour is highest. They are in slightly less dense catchments where the routes are longer, the trips-per-hour is lower, and the income per shift is more predictable shift over shift.

The population level says income drives retention. The cohort level says predictability drives retention. Both can be true; both are showing up in the data; we have been operating as if only the first is.

For two years our dispatch logic has optimised for what the population-level model said mattered. The longest-tenured riders may have been staying despite the dispatch logic, not because of it.

I'm not yet sure what this implies operationally. The clean answer would be a dispatch optimisation that allows for predictability bands. The honest answer is that we may have been quietly making the long-tenure rider experience worse for two years without seeing it in the topline metric we trusted.

The riders who stayed longest have been telling us this in quiet ways for a while. We just hadn't asked.
rows.col_6:
Original opener: A pattern in our rider attrition data over the last four quarters has been bothering me.

rows.col_3: Real-estate density
rows.col_4: Quick commerce is leasing, sourcing, and lease-back math with software inside.
rows.col_5:
Quick commerce in India is a leasing business with a delivery skin stretched over the top. Most of the public conversation still treats it as a tech business. That mistake is doing more damage than the people making it realise.

The thing that decides whether a pin code makes money is not the dispatch algorithm. It is whether the sourcing team got a 1,800-square-foot box at the right rent within 1.6 kilometres of enough households at the right basket size.

This is why the operating teams in this category, in India, look more like retail teams than tech teams. The headcount mix is heavier on real-estate, lease negotiation, and store ops. The board reviews are heavier on store productivity by catchment than on feature velocity by sprint. The metric that decides whether the business compounds quarter on quarter is not weekly active users. It is store productivity per pin code.

Most of the international playbook for this category was written for a SaaS-shaped reality. Indian quick commerce is not SaaS-shaped. It is rent-shaped.

The interesting work in this category for the next three years is not in the app. It is in the lease. The companies that figure out how to negotiate lease cycles faster than their competitors will compound. The companies still trying to win on feature velocity will not.

You do not build a quick-commerce moat in code. You build it in real estate.

rows.col_3: Open-source pre-peer-review
rows.col_4: I keep choosing the public, unfinished, exposed version of the work over the closed, polished, peer-reviewed one.
rows.col_5:
There is a version of every piece of work I am doing right now that is finished, polished, and waiting until peer review.

I keep choosing not to wait.

The Gravity Aging Hypothesis went up before peer review. The Temple data going up later this year will go up before peer review. Most of what I write on this platform is going up before I'm sure of it.

The argument for this approach: disagreement that arrives in public sharpens the work faster than disagreement that arrives in a journal twelve months later. The half-formed version of the hypothesis I published in November is meaningfully sharper today because three neuroscientists, two physicists, and a pulmonologist took the time to publicly disagree with parts of it.

The argument against: unfinished thinking gets read as finished thinking by readers who are not following closely. Headlines flatten 'open question' into 'Goyal claims.' That is a real and recurring cost.

I am not sure how to net the two against each other. I keep choosing the public version because the alternative is a closed-loop iteration that takes 18 to 24 months per round, and I am 47. The math on closed-loop iteration does not work for someone trying to do real work in the decades I have left of cognitive bandwidth.

This is not a defence. It is a description of why I made the call. Whether the call was good is something I won't know for two years.

I'd still rather get it wrong in public than be right too late.

rows.col_3: Next-billion blindspot
rows.col_4: India's next 200 million internet users won't be the upgrade lane — they will be the new design target.
rows.col_5:
India is going to add roughly two hundred million internet users in the next four years. Almost none of our consumer apps are designed for them.

The user we acquired in the last decade had a smartphone before she had savings. She had English before she had a salary. She had a friend who used the app before she did. Almost none of these is true for the next two hundred million.

The next user is on a phone two generations behind the median tester rig. Her data plan is a third the bandwidth. Her first language on the screen is not English. Her trust threshold for digital payment was set by a single bad UPI experience her cousin had three years ago.

Our apps assume otherwise. The category page leads with English. The refund flow needs three taps. The video previews assume bandwidth she does not have. The customer-support handover assumes a chat language nobody on her side writes in.

None of these is hard to fix. They are unattended because the user we are designing for is already in the funnel. The user we are about to meet has not entered it.

The companies that compound in Indian consumer for the rest of the decade will be the ones that treat the next two hundred million as the design target, not the upgrade lane.

The growth is not in the user we already have.

rows.col_3: Stepping-down identity sunk cost
rows.col_4: It took me four months to write the letter. The hard part wasn't the decision — it was the first sentence.
rows.col_5:
It took me four months to write the letter announcing I was stepping away as Group CEO.

The decision wasn't the hard part. The decision was clear by August.

What was hard was that I could not find the first sentence. Every draft started with the company. 'Eternal has had a strong year.' 'Eternal is at an inflection point.' Eternal, Eternal, Eternal.

None of those was the real opening. The real opening was that I had spent eighteen years being a person whose first sentence was the company. I did not yet know how to write a sentence as someone else.

This is what people miss when they talk about founder transitions. They talk about timing, succession, valuation, board dynamics. Those are real. The actual cost is the part nobody puts on a slide — the rewrite of the sentence you start with when you introduce yourself.

I am two months in now. Some days I still say 'we' about a Q4 decision I had no role in. Albinder is making calls I would have made differently and choosing to learn that this is fine. The calendar emptied exactly when I expected it to. The work to be done was not on the calendar.

It was on the sentence.

I haven't found mine yet.

rows.col_3: Consumer product mirrors org chart
rows.col_4: Every consumer product is a mirror of the organisation that built it — including the seams nobody planned.
rows.col_5:
Every consumer product is a mirror of the organisation that built it. Including the seams nobody planned.

In 1968 a computer scientist named Melvin Conway made an observation that has held up across half a century of software: any system's design ends up reflecting the communication structure of the team that built it. Engineers call it Conway's Law.

It is not just a software law. It applies to the feel of a consumer product — its speed, its tone, its instincts.

The product you are building right now is not primarily shaped by your strategy or your tech. It is shaped by who talks to whom inside your team, where information flows, and where it gets stuck. Which teams share language. Which teams operate in different vocabularies for the same concept.

In a consumer business this shows up in concrete ways. The handoff between marketing and product creates the mismatch in voice between your ad copy and your in-app onboarding. The handoff between product and ops creates the mismatch between what the customer is promised on the screen and what the rider can actually deliver. The handoff between ops and customer support creates the lag in refund cycles that quietly damages retention.

None of these are failures of talent. They are structural outcomes of who is in which Slack channel.

You ship your org chart. Whether you mean to or not.

What this implies in practice: when something feels off in the product, the instinct is to fix the product. The product is the evidence. The problem is upstream, in how the team communicates. Pull up the org chart and look at where the boundaries between teams sit. Then look at where the seams in the product show. They line up.

You can't unsee it. And once you see it, you can design for it.

rows.col_3: App retention mirrors team rituals
rows.col_4: The retention curve of a consumer app is shaped less by strategy than by which weekly ritual the team protects.
rows.col_5:
The retention curve of a consumer app is a mirror of the weekly ritual the team protects.

This is the part of Conway's Law that does not get talked about. The org chart shapes the structure. The weekly ritual shapes the discipline. And it is the discipline, not the structure, that decides whether the app gets opened on a Wednesday in October.

Here is what I mean. The teams I have watched produce the strongest retention curves all have one weekly meeting they will not skip. Some call it ops review. Some call it customer review. The ones that work share three properties: the meeting goes through real customer cases, not slide summaries. It surfaces the small frictions that no other meeting touches. And the founder is in it most of the time.

The teams that produce weaker retention curves either skip this meeting when something more urgent comes up, or they have a version that is too senior to discuss the real friction, or they have decoupled the founder from the customer in a way that takes 18 months to undo.

In Conway's terms: the retention curve is the residue of the ritual the team chose to protect. The ritual is the communication structure that touches the customer most often.

You can't fix retention with a feature. You can fix retention with a meeting that happens every week, runs the same way, and never gets cancelled when something flashier shows up on the calendar.

Most consumer founders I know cancelled this meeting somewhere between Series B and Series C. Most of them are now wondering why retention got harder to defend.

rows.col_3: Company under successor mirrors handoff seams
rows.col_4: Watching the company under Albinder is showing me which seams of the org I had been holding together by force of personal involvement.
rows.col_5:
Every company is a mirror of the founder who built it. Including the seams the founder had been holding together by force of personal involvement.

I have been not-CEO for two months. The handoff has been clean by every public measure. Internally, what I am noticing is more interesting than the numbers.

There are decisions that used to flow because I was the connecting tissue between two leaders who didn't talk to each other in any structured way. Those decisions still happen. They take longer. They take more meetings. They sometimes do not happen at all.

This is not a critique of the team. It is a description of the seams I had been holding together without naming. For eighteen years I had been compensating for handoffs that did not exist — and the compensation had been invisible to everyone, including me, because it happened in private conversations and side messages that nobody put on a workflow.

Conway was right and his law has a footnote. The footnote is that founders sometimes patch the seams of their org chart with their own time and never document the patch. The day the founder steps back, the patch becomes visible because it stops happening.

Albinder is doing the harder work right now of finding which seams need a real handoff process, which need a new role, and which were never seams at all.

What I'm sitting with: I should have made those patches visible while I was still in the seat. I didn't. The cost of not having done that is being paid by the team for the next two quarters.

rows.col_3: Boring discipline / Tuesday review
rows.col_4: The compounding decisions in a consumer business sit on a Tuesday review, not a strategy offsite.
rows.col_5:
The decisions that compound in a consumer business almost never look like the ones a strategy offsite produces.

The offsite gets the photographer, the deck, and the press. The decisions that compound get the Tuesday review with bad lighting and an agenda the team is occasionally too tired to argue about.

On a Tuesday review the boring decisions happen. The refund-cycle is dropping by twelve seconds — ship the new flow or wait. The drop-off rate on the third onboarding screen is up 1.4 points in tier-two cities — what changed. Two pin codes have a courier integration timeout silently failing 0.6 percent of orders — fix this week.

None of those decisions is exciting. None of them survives a slide. All of them compound into the retention curve.

The companies that look durable five years in are the ones whose Tuesday reviews got sharper, faster, and more honest, year over year. The ones that look fragile are the ones where the offsite got better and the Tuesday review got skipped.

You can't market a Tuesday review. You can't put one in an investor deck. You can put a calendar invite for it on the operating team's week and refuse to move it.

That, in my experience, is most of the difference between consumer companies that compound and consumer companies that don't.

rows.col_3: First week post-CEO conversations
rows.col_4: My calendar refilled with conversations the CEO chair had been blocking from happening.
rows.col_5:
In my first week not being CEO, three founders I respect called me unprompted to ask the kind of question they hadn't been able to ask while I was still in the chair.

One was about a category bet they had been wrong on for two years. One was about a co-founder dynamic that wasn't working. One was about whether they were the right person to keep running the thing they had built.

None of those questions could have happened on a board call, on Slack, or in any room with witnesses.

What surprised me was not that they called. It was the volume of conversation I had been blocking from happening just by being in a public-company seat. The CEO badge does this thing where it makes other founders edit themselves around you. They want your time. They don't want your shareholder letter.

I had braced for the calendar to empty. I had not braced for it to refill with the conversations I had been quietly missing for years.

I'm still working out the second-order implication. Part of me is grateful. Part of me wonders how much of the founder-to-founder honesty in this country has been quietly held back by the seats founders have been sitting in.

What I am sure of is that the calendar I have now is harder to defend on a slide and easier to defend to myself.

rows.col_3: Structural vs subsidised QC
rows.col_4: Most quick-commerce networks in India today are two businesses stacked under one brand and the customer can't tell which one is delivering her order.
rows.col_5:
Most quick-commerce networks in India today are running two business models stacked under one brand. The customer cannot tell the difference. The investor often can't either.

Model A is structural. The dark store sits in a catchment dense enough that the unit math closes at the speed offered. The rider is moving fast because the system was designed for fast.

Model B is subsidised. The store is in a pin code where the basket is too small or the density too thin to make the math close at the promised SLA. The speed is being paid for somewhere — by the operating P&L, by the rider's effective hourly, by investor patience.

From the customer's screen the two are identical. Same app. Same ten minutes. Same packet at the door.

From the inside, the two are completely different businesses. Model A compounds. Model B converts to either Model A or to a closed store.

Most of the public conversation about quick commerce conflates the two networks. It treats the average as the truth. The average lies. A 1,800-store network running half-and-half looks like one business and is structurally two.

The operators who survive the next two years in this category are the ones already converting Model B stores to Model A — by adding category depth, raising AOV, or closing the stores where the math will not bend.

That work is invisible from the customer's screen. It is the only work that matters.

rows.col_3: Blinkit crossover taught us
rows.col_4: Blinkit crossing Zomato in NOV was a Conway moment — the org chart we'd built for one business produced a second business we hadn't planned.
rows.col_5:
Blinkit's NOV crossed Zomato's NOV this year. The clean explanation is that quick commerce as a category compounded faster than food delivery did. That explanation is true.

There is a less clean explanation that I have been thinking about more.

For most of the last five years our org chart, our reviews, our investor narrative, and our internal language treated Zomato as the platform and Blinkit as the bet. That was a real choice and it had real consequences.

What it did was create two operating cultures inside one company. The Zomato culture became more disciplined, more margin-focused, more cautious. The Blinkit culture became more experimental, more catchment-driven, more comfortable with capital intensity. The two cultures shared a parent and not much else.

Conway's Law says you ship your org chart. We did. The org chart said Zomato is the parent and Blinkit is the child, and the businesses we built reflected that hierarchy. The market then decided that the child grew up faster.

This is one of the more uncomfortable lessons from this quarter for me. The strategy was right. The org chart was right for one phase and wrong for the next. The crossover was visible in the org chart eighteen months before it was visible in NOV. We just didn't read the chart.

I'm still working out what the cleaner version of this lesson is. The one I'm certain of: when the org chart and the strategy diverge, the org chart wins.

rows.col_3: Hire for distance from comfort
rows.col_4: The hires who survive the first three years of an Indian consumer company aren't the credentialed ones — they're the ones who've moved through real friction.
rows.col_5:
The single best filter I have used for early hires in any company I have built or backed is the candidate's distance from comfort.

This is not a sentimental filter. It is a practical one.

In an early-stage Indian consumer company, the work is full of weeks where the answer to the next question is not in any deck and not in any podcast. The candidate has to figure out how to get a kitchen partner approval in a tier-3 city where neither party speaks the other's first language. Why a refund flow is silently failing for one cohort in Lucknow but not in Pune. How to negotiate rider parking at a mall at 11 p.m. on a Sunday before a launch.

None of that work is taught at any school. The credential gap between a top-tier IIT graduate and a small-town engineer who built her own way out of two unrelated industries is, in my experience of about a hundred consumer hires, not predictive of who solves these problems faster.

Distance from comfort is. Candidates who have moved through real friction in their own life have a calibration I cannot teach in any onboarding. They know what is recoverable and what isn't. They don't panic at the third unknown.

The credentialed candidate is often easier to hire. They are also often the wrong person for the work that defines whether the company survives its first three years.

They don't panic at the third unknown. That is the filter.

rows.col_3: Temple data point
rows.col_4: A year of Temple data taught me that the data outside your hypothesis is often where the hypothesis breaks.
rows.col_5:
After a year of wearing Temple, the data point I keep coming back to is one I was not tracking.

I had built the Gravity Aging Hypothesis around posture and time-on-feet. Cerebral blood flow as a posture phenomenon was the variable I was watching.

What kept showing up in the noise was something else. The largest day-over-day swings in cerebral blood flow happened in the first thirty minutes of focused cognitive work — specifically, sitting down to write something hard.

This is one observation, on one body, on a non-clinical instrument. It is not a hypothesis. It is barely an anecdote.

But it has done something to how I think about Continue Research's next phase. The original hypothesis treated cerebral blood flow as primarily a posture phenomenon. The data is hinting that posture is one input and cognition is another, and that the relative weight of the two is not what I assumed.

The discipline I am trying to learn: the data you collect with the lens you brought is not the data that matters. Most of the interesting science I have read this year started with someone noticing something they were not looking for and following it.

For consumer founders this is the same lesson in different clothes. The dashboard you built measures the variables you imagined as important when you built it. The question that decides whether the business compounds is usually about a variable nobody put on the dashboard.

I am not yet sure where this Temple observation leads. I am sure the lens that found it was the wrong lens for the original question.

rows.col_3: Restraint as a board-level non-negotiable
rows.col_4: The strongest move a consumer-company CFO can make in front of a board is to draw the line on which categories aren't going to be funded this year.
rows.col_5:
The best thing I heard this quarter? A consumer-company CFO telling her board exactly which categories the company was not going to fund.

This was after the FY27 planning review. The board pointed at the slide showing six potential expansion categories and asked the obvious question: why are we only funding two.

She didn't give the standard pitch about prioritisation or operating leverage. She made it a clean unit-economics conversation.

She told them: 'I will fund any category you can show me a path to a 4 percent margin in within six quarters. I will not fund any category that requires us to subsidise the unit math while we wait for the slide to look better.'

Then she offered the trade. She showed them how the two funded categories would compound capital efficiently for three years and exit Year 4 throwing off cash that would fund the next set of category bets without dilution.

She drew a hard line on the four categories the board had been hoping to greenlight. The line was not 'these are bad categories.' The line was 'these don't have a unit-math path on the timeline you're being asked to underwrite.'

That is exactly the pushback you want from a CFO. Not the discipline of refusal. The discipline of which growth is being refused, and why.

The companies that compound for the next decade in Indian consumer will be the ones whose CFOs get this kind of conversation right with their boards. Most are still negotiating the wrong line. The line is not how much we spend. The line is which growth we will say no to.

rows.col_3: Founder/board non-negotiables on speed
rows.col_4: The best founder pushback I've watched this year was on the question 'can we promise faster?'
rows.col_5:
The best thing I heard at a board meeting this quarter? A founder telling her board: 'I will not promise the customer something the network cannot structurally deliver.'

This was after a long discussion about whether the company could promise an even faster delivery time as a marketing bet. The board's intuition was reasonable. The category was crowded. Speed was the visible variable. A faster promise might unlock share.

She didn't push back with a passionate speech about brand promises. She made it a unit-economics conversation.

She told them: 'If we promise the customer eight minutes and the network can structurally deliver eight minutes, that's a marketing decision. If we promise the customer eight minutes and the network can deliver eight minutes only by squeezing rider hourly rates, dispatching to fail riders, or running on a subsidy somebody is going to wake up and turn off, we have not made a marketing decision. We have made a balance-sheet decision the marketing line is going to take credit for.'

Then she offered the trade. Slower promise, structurally deliverable, refundable in a clean SLA. Lower marketing punch, durable retention.

The board approved the slower number.

This is the kind of pushback a founder should be giving the board on every speed conversation in this category. The customer will reward the structural number. The customer will punish the subsidised one when it eventually turns off.

You can promise what your network can do. You can't promise what your network can be subsidised into doing. The two look identical for a quarter and diverge for a decade.

rows.col_3: A board moment he didn't handle well
rows.col_4: On the Q3 earnings call I gave the rehearsed answer. The truer one was sitting in my notes.
rows.col_5:
The worst thing I did at a board interaction this year was give the rehearsed answer when the truer one was in my notes.

This was on the Q3 earnings call. An analyst asked which decision had driven the 5.3 percent margin on food delivery — the highest in our history. I had a clean three-line answer ready. Category maturity. Operating leverage. Disciplined capital allocation.

I gave the clean answer. The line moved on.

What I should have said: there was no decision. The margin is the residue of about a hundred small no's spread over six quarters. None of those no's was heroic. Most of them I forgot inside a week.

The truer answer would have been more useful to the analyst, more credible to the long-term shareholder, and more honest. I gave the rehearsed answer because earnings calls reward stories that compress into a quote.

This is one of the small failures I am noticing more often since stepping back. The structures we build for communicating with capital have a shape. The shape rewards the clean line. The clean line is sometimes the right answer. It is more often the easier one.

I am still figuring out what the founder's job is on these calls. Not to perform. Not to sell. Maybe to occasionally not give the line the team has rehearsed and instead say what I actually believe.

It is harder than it sounds. I am working on it. I do not yet do it well.

rows.col_4: The hire who survives the messiest week of the year is rarely the one with the cleanest resume.
rows.col_5:
The hire who survives the messiest operating week of the year in an Indian consumer company is rarely the one with the cleanest resume.

It is usually the one whose own life had a year nothing on her resume could prepare her for.

This is not an argument against credentials. The credential gets the candidate into the room. It is an argument against the credential as the deciding filter once the candidate is in the room.

The work in early-stage Indian consumer is full of weeks where the answer to the next question is not on a slide. The candidate has to figure out how to negotiate a kitchen partner approval where neither party speaks the other's first language. Why a refund flow is silently failing for one cohort in Lucknow and not in Pune. How to get rider parking at a mall at 11 p.m. on a Sunday.

None of that is taught at any school. Distance from comfort is the trait that predicts who will solve it without panicking. Candidates who have moved through real friction in their own life have a calibration I cannot teach in any onboarding.

The credentialed candidate is often easier to hire. They are also often the wrong person for the work.

The deciding filter for early hires is not what is on the resume. It is what is missing from it that the candidate had to find out for herself.

rows.col_3: Rider conversation reframed product
rows.col_4: A rider asked us to ship something for his wife. Our product process had been written without his household in mind.
rows.col_5:
On a recent ride-along a rider asked me why the app, which lets him see his earnings to the rupee, doesn't let his wife see them.

He explained that when his earnings dropped one week, his wife thought he had been spending money on something he wasn't. The opacity to her was the source of an argument they had at home that lasted three days.

It was not a feature request. It was a description of a friction in his life that our product was creating without anyone on our team knowing.

I have sat in product reviews for eighteen years. We have built family-share features for customers. We had not built them for riders. The reason was not technical. The reason was that the rider had not been imagined as a person who has a household to be transparent with.

That is what class invisibility looks like inside a product backlog. Not malice. A failure of imagination at the moment somebody types up the ticket.

What I have committed to in the next quarter: in the next four product reviews I sit in on, the first question I am going to ask is not what this builds for the customer. It is what this builds for, or against, the rider's household.

The thing he asked for is on the December backlog. The bigger thing — the question we should have been asking on every backlog — is what I am trying to fix.

It is overdue.
