    {
      "label": "Revenue truth",
      "definition": "The real numbers that emerge when marketing sales and finance versions are reconciled. Most orgs don't have this.",
      "frequency": 7,
      "id": "vocab_fd3e1745f3",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Attribution politics",
      "definition": "Attribution models as political documents. Configured by whoever touched them last to tell their story.",
      "frequency": 6,
      "id": "vocab_975527617e",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Institutional memory half-life",
      "definition": "How fast marketing's institutional memory decays. Typically 18 months — one CMO tenure cycle.",
      "frequency": 3,
      "id": "vocab_29a8341881",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Model volatility",
      "definition": "When performance evaluation swings not from actual work changing but from the measurement model changing.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_ff0e5e0a3e",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Decision infrastructure",
      "definition": "Influence that helps enterprise buyers decide not discover. Right voice unblocks deals.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_a9425b8f9a",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Narrative control",
      "definition": "The function closest to revenue controls the story. Sales controls because CEO trusts sales numbers.",
      "frequency": 4,
      "id": "vocab_ea51a09aa3",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Lagging output judgment",
      "definition": "Being evaluated on results from a strategy you've already replaced. Pipeline from Q1 decisions lands in Q3.",
      "frequency": 3,
      "id": "vocab_e6335b070e",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Full-funnel stitch",
      "definition": "Connecting top-of-funnel metrics to actual pipeline and revenue. Where truth gets exposed.",
      "frequency": 3,
      "id": "vocab_a5f96961bf",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Vendor measurement incentive",
      "definition": "Vendors have financial incentive to keep measurement at top of funnel where their numbers look best.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_c7b61db216",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Creative destruction",
      "definition": "Killing your own revenue source before disruption does. Intercom's $60M ARR sacrifice.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_d1b736a04e",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Moneyball CMO",
      "definition": "Deepinder's content series. Real conversations with CMOs about what's working and what's broken.",
      "frequency": 3,
      "id": "vocab_c82bf115d7",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Dashboard repricing",
      "definition": "The $285B SaaS wipeout. Market repricing every tool that stops at showing answers instead of executing.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_f4f2da8ff3",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Excavation tenure",
      "definition": "CMO spending entire tenure reverse-engineering predecessor's decisions instead of building own foundation.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_dc7541928c",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Impossible choice",
      "definition": "What happens when someone exposes full-funnel truth: admit junk leads kill top source explain to board. Villain treatment.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_9706fb4b9d",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Genius-fraud oscillation",
      "definition": "The CMO emotional cycle. Genius when pipeline is green. Fraud when it's not. Both driven by lagging metrics.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_c10af329f0",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Boardroom anxiety",
      "definition": "The need to see motion that pushes execs into tools they didn't choose for problems they didn't diagnose.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_79fbf4e528",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Category capture",
      "definition": "Getting on the shortlist before RFP. Brand's real job in enterprise. MrBeast guarantees Salesforce makes the list.",
      "frequency": 1,
      "id": "vocab_d55be7c6f8",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Pain recognition speed",
      "definition": "How fast a prospect recognizes they have the problem you solve. Complex GTM = faster recognition.",
      "frequency": 2,
      "id": "vocab_2362b42497",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "Sinusoidal wave",
      "definition": "The emotional pattern of CMO life. Up when pipeline looks good down when it doesn't. Neither reflects actual performance.",
      "frequency": 1,
      "id": "vocab_3b4880bf87",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    },
    {
      "label": "System designed to lose",
      "definition": "When the org structure ensures the person in the role cannot succeed regardless of capability.",
      "frequency": 3,
      "id": "vocab_eda618b263",
      "type": "vocabulary",
      "node_type": "vocabulary"
    }

nodes.label: CMOs fired for measurement failure not marketing failure
nodes.stance: Shortest C-suite tenure. Not capability — attribution models are political documents.

nodes.label: Attribution models are political documents
nodes.stance: First-touch makes demand gen heroic. Last-touch makes SDR the engine. Configured by someone with agenda.

nodes.label: A number you can't explain is a number you can't defend
nodes.stance: 107% is dangerous when nobody knows how. Board raises targets for something never understood.

nodes.label: Marketing is the only function everyone thinks they're qualified for
nodes.stance: Nobody tells CFO how to structure balance sheet. But every exec has opinion on marketing.

nodes.label: The CMO seat is designed to fail
nodes.stance: Budget holder without lever control. Five mandates zero decision rights.

nodes.label: Revenue truth is the real problem
nodes.stance: Marketing sales finance each have different numbers. Nobody agrees which is real.

nodes.label: CROs control narrative because sales is closer to revenue
nodes.stance: When numbers conflict CEO trusts sales. CMO contradicted before finishing slide.

nodes.label: Software showing problems dying to software solving them
nodes.stance: $285B SaaS wipeout. Operators want agents that finish the job not dashboards.

nodes.label: Nobody gets promoted for stopping bad marketing
nodes.stance: Credit for campaigns launched. No credit for positioning that would've failed.

nodes.label: Build in public with substance not manufactured content
nodes.stance: Won't publish what AI could spin up in five minutes. Real operator conversations only.

nodes.label: Complex GTM motions need RevSure most
nodes.stance: More complex = faster pain recognition = bigger initial deal = faster expansion.

nodes.label: Capital is commodity sponsorship and distribution matter
nodes.stance: Most advice isn't context-aware. What matters is sponsorship and distribution.

nodes.label: Creative destruction requires courage not strategy
nodes.stance: Intercom killed $60M ARR. Honest word isn't creative destruction — it's courage.

nodes.label: AI visibility has 46x citation gap problem
nodes.stance: Grok cites 27%. ChatGPT cites 0.59%. Same brand same window. Optimization before measurement.

nodes.label: The unsexy orchestration layer is the real moat
nodes.stance: Data harmonization connectivity stitching insights. Not glamorous but without it you're shippable in 30 days.

nodes.label: Every 18 months institutional marketing memory gets wiped
nodes.stance: CMOs leave taking demand thesis and brand narrative. Next one excavates someone else's thinking.

nodes.label: CMO goes from leading to explaining in one meeting
nodes.stance: Senior executive pushes different view and energy changes. Authority shrinks when revenue impact feels abstract.

nodes.label: Vendor incentive keeps measurement at top of funnel
nodes.stance: Content syndication vendor's business model depends on marketing team never seeing full-funnel conversion.

nodes.label: CMOs not on rollercoaster — reading last quarter's weather
nodes.stance: Campaigns from January show in April pipeline. Board reviews every 90 days. She's watching pipeline she can't affect.

nodes.label: Motion rewarded over judgment in marketing
nodes.stance: Boards want motion. Teams want output. Incentive always to do more. Most valuable work is deciding what not to do.

nodes.label: Pipeline from decisions won't land for two quarters
nodes.stance: CMO killed low-performing channels. Smart call. But judged on lagging output of strategy already replaced.

nodes.label: Influence in enterprise is decision infrastructure
nodes.stance: Enterprise buyers don't lack content. They lack confidence. Right voice unblocks deals compresses cycles.

nodes.label: Most CMOs never lay their own foundation
nodes.stance: Spend entire tenure excavating predecessor's thinking. Never get to build their own.

nodes.label: Strong CROs ask one question that kills 80% of bad initiatives
nodes.stance: 'Has any buyer told you this is why they didn't sign?' Kills boardroom anxiety initiatives.

nodes.label: Brand gets you on the shortlist product wins the deal
nodes.stance: Before RFP there's quiet filtering. Nobody champions vendor they've never heard of. MrBeast guarantees Salesforce makes list.

nodes.label: AEO category has measurement problem not optimization problem
nodes.stance: Visibility tool tells where cited. Doesn't tell which citations connect to pipeline for your buyers.

nodes.label: Audiences have strong pattern recognition for manufactured content
nodes.stance: They can distinguish earned insight from AI-manufactured content. Middle ground is being squeezed out.

nodes.label: Protecting things that stopped working is the real pattern
nodes.stance: MQL definitions not revisited. Attribution showing marketing in best light. Tool nobody admits isn't working.

nodes.label: CEO call to replace marketing head — three questions
nodes.summary: CEO wanted to replace CMO. Three diagnostic questions exposed the CRO controls narrative because sales closer to revenue.

nodes.label: VP Marketing promoted Q1 fired Q3
nodes.summary: Attribution model credited her channels one quarter, didn't the next. She didn't change. The lens did.

nodes.label: CMO 107% pipeline but can't explain how
nodes.summary: $5B company CMO said 'we hit 107% but we don't know how.' Board raises targets. Replicating unknown at 120% scale.

nodes.label: CMO genius and fraud in same quarter
nodes.summary: Described job as sinusoidal wave of emotions. One day CFO gets her next quarter explaining spends.

nodes.label: Content syndication 12000 leads 50 in pipeline
nodes.summary: 'Happy or angry' — 99.6% rejection rate. Vendor's model depends on never seeing full-funnel.

nodes.label: Amisha rebuilding attribution — almost killed her team
nodes.summary: Power BI to Snowflake but manual pulls night before board. Marketing sales finance three versions. Revenue truth problem.

nodes.label: RevSure 4x ARR in FY2026
nodes.summary: 4x ARR. Two quarters added more than cumulative start. Found ICP: large enterprises complex GTM.

nodes.label: Intercom killed $60M ARR on purpose
nodes.summary: Eoghan McCabe moved 80% R&D to Fin. Creative destruction. Most teams protect last year's playbook.

nodes.label: $285 billion SaaS wipeout in one day
nodes.summary: Anthropic Claude Cowork. Salesforce Workday down 40%. Dashboard software dying to agent software.

nodes.label: CMO hired for execution fired for not mind-reading
nodes.summary: 80% searches end same way. Product needs education sales needs leads. Five mandates zero decision rights.

nodes.label: Moneyball CMO launch — no manufactured content
nodes.summary: One decision: no content AI could spin up. Real CMO conversations. Podcast and newsletter together.

nodes.label: Salesforce MrBeast Super Bowl ad — 12K word analysis
nodes.summary: Brand loyalty debate missed the point. Brand gets on shortlist. Product wins deal. Category capture timing.

nodes.label: Jason Beddall 10X content output — wrong metric
nodes.summary: 10X'd marketing content. Efficiency metrics up. Then automated outreach and everything broke.

nodes.label: CMO institutional memory wipe every 18 months
nodes.summary: Each CMO exit takes demand thesis and brand narrative. Next one reverse-engineers why MQL threshold is set that way.

nodes.label: Profound $96M raise and AEO 46x citation gap
nodes.summary: AEO grew from 7 to 150+ tools in 10 months. But 46x gap in citation rates across platforms.

nodes.label: Amisha on influencer marketing as decision infrastructure
nodes.summary: 'Not for distribution but for helping buyers reach a decision.' Right voice unblocks deals.

nodes.label: Amisha $50K event killed after two years zero prospects
nodes.summary: Built B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch. Guerrilla billboards in target account districts.

nodes.label: CMO seat designed to fail — authority never forms
nodes.summary: Budget holder without lever control. Senior exec pushes different view, CMO goes from leading to explaining.

nodes.label: CEO he doubled down on existing CMO instead of replacing
nodes.summary: After three diagnostic questions CEO kept the CMO. She's nailing it. Fix the system not the person.

nodes.label: Marketers job everyone has opinion on — finance comparison
nodes.summary: Nobody tells CFO how to structure balance sheet. But marketing gets that treatment daily.

nodes.label: Nobody gets promoted for stopping — the invisible work
nodes.summary: Killing rebrand when brand wasn't problem. Saying no to enterprise when product not ready. Telling CEO tagline means nothing.

nodes.label: RevSure solving unsexy data harmonization
nodes.summary: Data harmonization connectivity stitching. Not glamorous. But without it smart developer ships you in 30 days.

nodes.label: Operator Collective — capital is commodity
nodes.summary: Mallun Yen building fund around operator DNA. Sponsorship and distribution matter more than capital.

nodes.label: Opens with a real conversation or direct quote
nodes.rule: 'A CEO called me.' 'A CMO told us.' 'She described her job as.' Real people real exchanges.

nodes.label: Three-question rapid diagnostic
nodes.rule: Sequence of diagnostic questions exposing real problem. Questions do the arguing.

nodes.label: Uses devastating math as punchline
nodes.rule: 99.6% rejection. 0.4% conversion. 46x gap. $285B wiped. The math IS the argument.

nodes.label: Named protagonists with specific contexts
nodes.rule: Amisha Gandhi, Eoghan McCabe, Mallun Yen, Jason Beddall. Names create credibility and intimacy.

nodes.label: Reframes obvious explanation to structural cause
nodes.rule: 'Everyone thinks X. Here's what actually happens.' Reframe is always structural never personal.

nodes.label: CMO advocacy as throughline in every post
nodes.rule: Nearly every post advocates for CMOs. Consistent empathy for structurally disadvantaged role.

nodes.label: Competing mandates as dark comedy
nodes.rule: Product needs education sales needs leads CEO wants category board wants this quarter. Comedy of impossibility.

nodes.label: Selective long-form deep dives
nodes.rule: Most posts 200-300 words. Occasionally 10K+ analysis. When going deep goes extremely deep.

nodes.label: Direct quotes as story anchors
nodes.rule: 'I don't know whether happy or angry.' 'It almost killed me.' Quote IS the hook.

nodes.label: Ends on systemic insight not personal advice
nodes.rule: Closes by zooming to structural observation. Problem is never the person — it's the system.

nodes.label: Sinusoidal wave metaphor for CMO experience
nodes.rule: Genius one quarter fraud the next. Emotional oscillation tied to lagging pipeline metrics.

nodes.label: Flywheel framing for systemic problems
nodes.rule: 'This is the flywheel nobody talks about.' Self-reinforcing cycles of institutional failure.

nodes.label: Fix the system not the person
nodes.rule: Recurring close: replacing the person won't help if the system is designed to make them fail.

nodes.label: Weather metaphor for attribution lag
nodes.rule: Reading last quarter's weather asked why today's temperature is wrong. Campaigns from Jan show in April.

nodes.label: Excavation metaphor for CMO tenure
nodes.rule: Each new CMO excavates predecessor's thinking. Never lays their own foundation.

nodes.label: One-line structural punch
nodes.rule: 'That's the whole story.' 'That's rare.' 'The person who brings this data becomes the villain.' Minimal maximum-impact sentences.

nodes.label: Board review cycle as structural antagonist
nodes.rule: Board reviews every 90 days. Pipeline from decisions won't land for two quarters. Structural timing mismatch.

nodes.label: Impossible choice framing
nodes.rule: Admits junk leads. Kills top lead source. Explains to board why 12K leads produced 51 deals. Villain framing for truth-teller.

nodes.label: Moneyball CMO as content brand
nodes.rule: Series name creates recurring brand. Real conversations with real operators. Podcast plus newsletter.

nodes.label: Narrative control diagnosis
nodes.description: Function closest to revenue controls the story. CMO enters every meeting with number that gets contradicted.
nodes.applies_to: org politics, CMO advocacy, revenue alignment

nodes.label: Attribution as political weapon
nodes.description: Attribution models configured by whoever touched last. Whoever configures writes the narrative.
nodes.applies_to: marketing measurement, CMO survival, revenue ops

nodes.label: Unexplainable success as risk
nodes.description: Quarter you can't explain more dangerous than quarter you missed. Board assumes machine works raises targets.

nodes.label: Structural role failure
nodes.description: CMO fails not because of person but positioning. Budget without levers. Five mandates zero decision rights.
nodes.applies_to: org design, C-suite dynamics, CMO tenure

nodes.label: Funnel truth exposure
nodes.description: Top of funnel looks good. Truth appears at full-funnel stitch. 12K leads becomes 50 opportunities.
nodes.applies_to: demand gen, vendor management, pipeline quality

nodes.label: Dashboard to agent transition
nodes.description: SaaS showing problems replaced by agents solving them. $285B repricing.
nodes.applies_to: product strategy, SaaS evolution, AI agents

nodes.label: Self-cannibalization courage
nodes.description: Kill own revenue before someone else does. Intercom killed $60M. Most teams protect last year's playbook.

nodes.label: ICP clarity through pain recognition speed
nodes.description: More complex GTM = faster pain recognition = bigger deal = faster expansion.
nodes.applies_to: ICP definition, sales targeting, product-market fit

nodes.label: Institutional memory half-life
nodes.description: Every CMO exit wipes marketing's institutional memory. 18-month cycle of excavation and rebuilding.
nodes.applies_to: knowledge management, CMO transition, org design

nodes.label: Lagging output judgment trap
nodes.description: CMO judged on lagging output of strategy they already replaced. Smart decisions look bad for two quarters.

nodes.label: Vendor measurement incentive misalignment
nodes.description: Vendor's business model depends on customer never seeing full-funnel conversion. Financial incentive to keep measurement at top.

nodes.label: Model volatility vs performance volatility
nodes.description: VP experienced model volatility not performance volatility. Her work didn't change but the lens did.

nodes.label: Influence as decision infrastructure
nodes.description: Enterprise buyers don't lack content — they lack confidence. Influence helps them decide not discover.
nodes.applies_to: B2B marketing, influencer strategy, buyer journey

nodes.label: Motion vs judgment incentive
nodes.description: Incentive always to do more. Most valuable work is deciding what not to do. Invisible until compared.

nodes.label: Category capture timing
nodes.description: Brand doesn't win deal — gets on shortlist. Before RFP there's quiet filtering where nobody champions unknown vendor.
nodes.applies_to: brand strategy, competitive positioning, enterprise sales

nodes.label: Marketing's number vs sales's number
nodes.left: Marketing reports pipeline from attribution model
nodes.right: Sales reports from what actually closed

nodes.label: Hired for execution vs fired for mind-reading
nodes.left: Job description says launch campaigns build pipeline
nodes.right: Actual job is knowing which exec to disappoint this week

nodes.label: Attribution objectivity vs attribution politics
nodes.right: Political document configured by whoever touched it last

nodes.label: Explainable miss vs unexplainable hit
nodes.left: Quarter missed but diagnosable and fixable
nodes.right: Quarter hit 107% but nobody can explain how

nodes.label: Top of funnel celebration vs full-funnel truth
Dashboard software vs agent software
nodes.right: Solves problems agents finish the job

nodes.label: Manufactured content vs earned insight
nodes.right: Real CMO conversations about what's actually broken

nodes.label: Campaigns launched vs bad marketing stopped
nodes.left: Credit for volume motion pipeline numbers
nodes.right: Invisible value of what you prevented

nodes.label: Protecting playbook vs cannibalizing revenue
nodes.left: Protecting proven channels and MQL definitions
nodes.right: Killing your own revenue before someone else does

nodes.label: AEO optimization vs citation gap reality
nodes.right: 46x gap in citation rates same brand same window

nodes.label: Marketing as everyone's expertise vs finance as sacred
nodes.left: Every exec has opinion because they've consumed marketing
nodes.right: Nobody tells CFO how to structure balance sheet

nodes.label: Genius quarter vs fraud quarter
nodes.left: CFO finally gets her campaigns working pipeline green
nodes.right: Next quarter explaining spends watching pipeline she can't affect

nodes.label: Leading the conversation vs explaining the plan
nodes.left: CMO owns the room anchors in revenue impact
nodes.right: Senior exec pushes different view CMO shifts to defense

nodes.label: New CMO laying foundation vs excavating predecessor
nodes.left: Building your own demand thesis and brand narrative
nodes.right: Spending 6 months reverse-engineering why MQL threshold is set at X

nodes.label: Replacing the person vs fixing the system
nodes.left: New CMO same team same tools same culture same outcome
nodes.right: Doubling down on existing CMO after fixing the system

nodes.label: Revenue attribution at the top vs revenue truth at the bottom
nodes.left: Vanity metrics that make campaigns look good
nodes.right: Full-funnel stitching that shows which decisions drove revenue

nodes.label: Revenue truth
nodes.definition: The real numbers that emerge when marketing sales and finance versions are reconciled. Most orgs don't have this.

nodes.label: Attribution politics
nodes.definition: Attribution models as political documents. Configured by whoever touched them last to tell their story.

nodes.label: Institutional memory half-life
nodes.definition: How fast marketing's institutional memory decays. Typically 18 months — one CMO tenure cycle.

nodes.label: Model volatility
nodes.definition: When performance evaluation swings not from actual work changing but from the measurement model changing.

nodes.label: Decision infrastructure
nodes.definition: Influence that helps enterprise buyers decide not discover. Right voice unblocks deals.

nodes.label: Narrative control
nodes.definition: The function closest to revenue controls the story. Sales controls because CEO trusts sales numbers.

nodes.label: Lagging output judgment
nodes.definition: Being evaluated on results from a strategy you've already replaced. Pipeline from Q1 decisions lands in Q3.

nodes.label: Full-funnel stitch
nodes.definition: Connecting top-of-funnel metrics to actual pipeline and revenue. Where truth gets exposed.

nodes.label: Vendor measurement incentive
nodes.definition: Vendors have financial incentive to keep measurement at top of funnel where their numbers look best.

nodes.label: Creative destruction
nodes.definition: Killing your own revenue source before disruption does. Intercom's $60M ARR sacrifice.

nodes.label: Moneyball CMO
nodes.definition: Deepinder's content series. Real conversations with CMOs about what's working and what's broken.

nodes.label: Dashboard repricing
nodes.definition: The $285B SaaS wipeout. Market repricing every tool that stops at showing answers instead of executing.

nodes.label: Excavation tenure
nodes.definition: CMO spending entire tenure reverse-engineering predecessor's decisions instead of building own foundation.

nodes.label: Impossible choice
nodes.definition: What happens when someone exposes full-funnel truth: admit junk leads kill top source explain to board. Villain treatment.

nodes.label: Genius-fraud oscillation
nodes.definition: The CMO emotional cycle. Genius when pipeline is green. Fraud when it's not. Both driven by lagging metrics.

nodes.label: Boardroom anxiety
nodes.definition: The need to see motion that pushes execs into tools they didn't choose for problems they didn't diagnose.

nodes.label: Category capture
nodes.definition: Getting on the shortlist before RFP. Brand's real job in enterprise. MrBeast guarantees Salesforce makes the list.

nodes.label: Pain recognition speed
nodes.definition: How fast a prospect recognizes they have the problem you solve. Complex GTM = faster recognition.

nodes.label: Sinusoidal wave
nodes.definition: The emotional pattern of CMO life. Up when pipeline looks good down when it doesn't. Neither reflects actual performance.

nodes.label: System designed to lose
nodes.definition: When the org structure ensures the person in the role cannot succeed regardless of capability.

posts_extracted.txt.text:
--- POST 1 ---
"We hit 107% of our gross pipeline goals, but we don’t know how."

That's what CMO of over a $5B company told me on our first call. And this is very different than hearing, we hit 70% of our targets, and we need to do better, tell me how can you help.

He said, "It’s always a nice thing when marketing is hitting close to 107% of its gross pipeline goals. You set these goals based on a ton of math and guesstimations, and sure enough, with three days left in the business days left in the quarter, you hit the goal. That’s seems unrealistic that, how did we get here? how do we repeat this?"

Every CMO will recognize this because they have all been in the QBR where the numbers are green, CEO is happy, CFO is willing to give more budget, but nobody in the room can explain which specific decisions made it green.

The danger isn't the quarter you can't explain. It's the quarter after.

Because the board sees 107% and assumes the machine works. So they raise targets. Now you need to replicate something you never understood in the first place, but at 120% scale.
- You set next quarter's goals based on results you can't attribute.
- You fund the channels that happened to be running when pipeline showed up.
- You starve the ones that weren't visible at the right moment.

Every quarter, the gap between your numbers and your understanding of those numbers gets wider.

What I respect about this particular CMO is what he did next. He didn't take the win and move on. He said, "I know what I don't know."

That's rare. Most leaders in his position do one of three things. They try to hack together dashboards internally. They assign some analyst to reverse-engineer attribution after the fact. Or they just choose to fly blind and hope the next quarter is green too.

The ones who actually solve it are the ones who treat the 107% quarter not as a success story but as a warning.

Because a number you can't explain is a number you can't defend. And the first time the board asks why you missed after four quarters of hitting, "we don't know how we did it before either" is not an answer that keeps you in the room.

--- POST 2 ---
"I don't know whether I should be happy or angry."

That's what a CMO told us when we showed us that her most promising campaign had only 0.4% conversion rate!

So, they signed up with a content syndication vendor, who generated leads for them, this vendor generated over 12,000 leads, got a bigger contract and made the top of the funnel look very impressive.

Then they looked at the funnel with RevSure. They had around 50 of those leads in the pipeline, 99.6% rejection rate. For every 240 leads they celebrated, one became a real opportunity. The other 239 were names in a database that would never buy anything.

Now, the vendor knows this, but their entire business model depends on the marketing team never seeing full-funnel conversion. The vendor has a financial incentive to keep measurement at the top. The marketing team has a reporting incentive. Everyone benefits from the lie continuing.

When someone finally sees the full funnel, they face an impossible choice. Admit you celebrated junk leads. Kill your top lead source. Explain to the board why 12,260 leads produced 51 deals. The person who brings this data to the room becomes the villain.

This CMO decided to bite the bullet, she saved her marketing dollars and directed them to other sources.

She wasn't happy or angry, she was both. Happy because she finally had the truth. Angry because the truth had been one query away for 1 year.

--- POST 3 ---
A CEO called me last year. He wanted to replace his marketing head. Asked if I knew someone.

I've heard this call before. Pipeline is weak, need someone who could deliver. Different CEO, same story, same conclusion: the person is the problem.

I asked three questions.
- Who told you marketing isn't working? The CRO.
- Do marketing and sales agree on pipeline numbers? Never.
- When the numbers conflict, who do you trust? Sales.

That's the whole story.

The CRO controls the narrative because sales is closer to revenue. The CMO shows up to every meeting with a number that gets contradicted before she finishes her slide. She spends more time defending marketing's right to exist than actually running marketing.

She had made smart budget calls six months ago, killed low-performing channels, and shifted spend. But pipeline from those decisions won't land for two more quarters. And she's being judged on the lagging output of a strategy she already replaced.

I asked him what would change if the new CMO joins. He told me, "Nothing, she'd get the same team, same tools, same culture."

So I explained to him that he wasn’t facing a CMO problem, they had designed a system that made the CMO lose.

Eventually, he decided to double down on her. I spoke to him a few weeks ago, the CMO is nailing in her role there. Funny what happens when you fix the system instead of replacing the person.

--- POST 4 ---
A VP of Marketing I know got promoted in Q1, but she was put on a performance improvement plan in Q3.

What changed?

One quarter the attribution model credited her channels. The next quarter, it didn't. She didn't change but the attribution logic did.

B2B attribution models are not objective measurement systems. They're political documents. They tell whatever story the last person who configured them wanted to tell.

- First-touch models make demand gen look heroic.
- Last-touch models make SDR look like the entire engine.
- Multi-touch models spread credit so thin nobody looks good and nobody can be held accountable for anything.

The VP who felt like she was winning one quarter and drowning the next wasn't experiencing performance volatility. She was experiencing model volatility.

Her actual work didn't change, but the lens did she was being evaluated changed. So, until companies stop confusing the lens with reality, they'll keep promoting and firing the same people for the same invisible reasons.

--- POST 5 ---
"It almost killed me and my team."

Amisha Gandhi was rebuilding attribution at a previous company from scratch and it was breaking her team.

They had everything from power BI to Snowflake. But they were doing manual pulls the night before every board meeting, triple-checking every number and walking in still not sure whether leadership would trust what she was showing.

The problem wasn't data, she had plenty of it. Marketing had their version, sales had theirs and finance had a third. Nobody agreed on which one was real, so nobody left with a decision.

They had a revenue truth problem.

When she finally got the foundation right, her team found something nobody had been looking for. A segment nobody was targeting was converting faster than anyone expected. Sales confirmed it. One small campaign. It became its own business line.

She built the system to prove marketing's value. It ended up finding new revenue.

Amisha has been CMO at Tipalti, built B2B influencer marketing at SAP from scratch when no enterprise company was touching it, and has run marketing from every seat at the table.

In this episode of Moneyball CMO, we get into:

- The $50K event she killed after two years of zero real prospects
- How she convinced SAP to fund a channel nobody in enterprise believed in
- Guerrilla marketing: billboards in target account business districts, outflanking NetSuite at their own conference
- Why most CMOs have a revenue truth problem and don't know it
- What the next CMO actually needs to look like

Full podcast link in the comments.

--- POST 6 ---
The CMO has the shortest average tenure of any C-suite role. Most people think they know why. They don't.

We blame the talent, strategy, or alignment. But here's what actually happens.

Every time a CMO exits, they take two things with them. The demand generation thesis. And the brand narrative. The logic of why this market, this message, this channel mix.

What stays behind is the CRM configuration, the lead scoring model, the channel weights - all outputs of decisions nobody wrote down. Built during a busy quarter by someone who's now gone. Configured the way it is because that's what the last rev ops person knew how to do.

The next CMO inherits that system and spends the first six months trying to reverse-engineer it. Why is the MQL threshold set at that value? Why does this campaign attribute to first-touch and that one to last? Why is the LinkedIn budget four times the search budget?

Nobody knows. The person who decided is gone.

Most CMOs never fully figure it out. So they do the only thing they can. They add their own layer of decisions on top of what they inherited.

By the time they're actually being assessed on what they've built, they realize they've spent the entire tenure excavating someone else's thinking. They never got to lay their own foundation.

This is the flywheel nobody talks about.

CMOs don't keep failing because the role attracts bad talent. They keep failing because every 18 months the institutional memory of how marketing arrived at what it is gets wiped completely clean.

And the next person starts digging from the same spot, before they also leave.

--- POST 7 ---
Marketers have the job everyone has an opinion on.

Every CEO, CFO, and CRO has an opinion on marketing. Which makes sense, they’ve been on the receiving end of it their whole lives. But somehow that turns into thinking they’re qualified to judge it.

Nobody does this with finance or engineering. No one walks into a meeting and tells the CFO how to structure a balance sheet just because they’ve looked at one before.

But marketing gets that treatment every single day. The CMO is the only C-suite executive whose expertise is routinely overridden by people whose only qualification is being a consumer.

And over time, it changes how a CMO operates.

They stop bringing the raw version of what they’re seeing in the market. Instead, they bring the version they know will land well in the room. The channel that doesn't feel impressive in a board deck gets cut. The message that actually resonates with buyers but sounds too simple in a leadership meeting never ships.

On paper, it looks like the company just has different opinions about marketing. In reality, it’s losing the clearest signal it has about what customers actually think.

So when a CMO underperforms, companies often assume it’s a marketing problem.

What they actually have is a room full of consumers who confused familiarity with expertise, and a CMO who learned very quickly that telling them the truth was a faster path out the door than telling them what they wanted to hear.

--- POST 8 ---
A CMO I know described her job to me once as feeling like a genius and a fraud in the same quarter.

I've heard that from almost every marketing leader I've spoken to in the last four years. Most people hear that and think, marketing leadership is emotionally demanding.

On one day, she gets excited that finally the CFO is getting her and in the next quarter she has to explain her spends to the leadership. It's a sinusoidal wave of emotions coming from how pipeline looks that quarter.

I hear it and think: something is wrong with what she's measuring. This is what happens with CMOs.
- The campaigns they ran in January show up in pipeline in April.
- The brand work they invested in last year closes as revenue this year.
- The SDR motion they rebuilt in Q1 hits its stride in Q3.

But their board reviews them every 90 days.

So when things look good, they're seeing the echo of decisions they made six months ago. But when things look bad, they're watching a pipeline she can no longer affect. It's already in motion.

So, my friend is not on a rollercoaster.

She's reading last quarter's weather and being asked why today's temperature is wrong.

The CMOs who appear steady aren't necessarily better operators. They've figured out how to stop letting a broken feedback loop tell them who they are.

--- POST 9 ---
Conversations with Amisha are always grounding for me.

She’s been an advisor in how we’ve thought about the product, the market, and our long-term direction - so having her on the Moneyball CMO podcast was genuinely special.

One idea she shared that really stayed with me, "Influencer marketing in enterprise isn’t there for distribution but for helping buyers reach to a decision."

Enterprise buyers don’t lack content. They’re already educated, well-informed, and already part of the buyer journey, because they are considering your product for solving a problem that is already bothering them.

What they struggle with is decisioning, they are thinking about the risk, hesitating about the internal alignment they have to do - they simply don’t feel confident enough.

That’s where influence actually works, as decision infrastructure. For any B2B marketer working for influencer marketing they should aim for:
- Building trust, not reach
- Attach influence to GTM moments, not just campaigns
- Choose credibility over popularity - focus on trusted operators your ICP already respects
- Plan how to embed it across sales, exec, and customer motions
- Measure decision movement, not vanity metrics like engagement or impressions

The right voice unblocks a deal, compresses cycles, aligns a buying committee or makes a buyer feel safer moving forward.

That’s where influence works in enterprise.

This is just a first glimpse of what she shared on the pod. Full episode coming soon, stay tuned!

--- POST 10 ---
Intercom killed $60M in ARR, on purpose.

Eoghan McCabe, CEO of Intercom recently wrote about his decision as the only way of saving SaaS - to cannibalise itself before someone else does. But I think there is a lesson here for everyone.

Intercom moved 80% of R&D to a product that was single digit revenue generator - Fin, when it was nothing. He calls it creative destruction. I think the more honest word is just: courage.

I've spent four years sitting in on marketing leadership conversations. And the pattern I keep seeing isn't that teams lack good tools or smart people. It's that they're protecting things that stopped working.

The MQL definition that hasn't been revisited in two years. The attribution model that shows marketing in the best light but can't answer the CFO's actual question. The tool they bought in 2022 that three people know how to use and nobody wants to admit isn't working.

Every one of those has a history. Someone fought for the budget. Someone's identity is wrapped up in it. So instead of replacing it, you add another layer on top - another integration, another dashboard, another AI feature on top of a broken foundation.

Intercom's story reads like a big dramatic transformation. But I think what Eoghan is actually describing is just the willingness to have the uncomfortable conversation; with your board, your team, yourself - that the thing you built isn't the thing you need anymore.

I see CMOs who do this. It is never easy and it always costs something in the short-term. But the ones who do it are almost always ahead 18 months later.

The ones who don't are still in the same meeting, defending the same broken model, wondering why the new AI tool didn't fix it.

Image credit: Eoghan McCabe

--- POST 11 ---
When I started building in public last year, I made one decision - I will not publish any manufactured ‘thought leadership’ content, which any AI tool - could spin up in five minutes.

Instead, I launched the Moneyball CMO series. Real conversations with CMOs running marketing at large brands- What's working inside their orgs, what's broken and what proving ROI looks like when you're accountable to the board.
I felt that these conversations deserved more than one format. That is why the podcast and newsletter started together.

A month in, here's what I've learned:
- People aren't overwhelmed by content. But they don’t like to see ChatGPT manufactured or unearned content. So, if you have not lived it, its is better to shut it.
- Audiences have strong pattern recognition now - they can distinguish if it is earned insight.
- We're entering a split market. Machine-generated commodity on one end. Authenticated expertise on the other. And the middle - polished, safe, detached content without lived specificity - is getting squeezed out.

--- POST 12 ---
The CMO seat at a B2B SaaS is the only C-suite role designed to fail.

The issue is rarely the capability of the person in the role. It comes from how the role is positioned inside the company.

Marketing becomes everyone’s side responsibility. Finance owns finance. Sales owns sales. But marketing is the only function where the budget holder does not fully control the levers that determine success.

Without a clear line between marketing activity and company priorities, a CMO’s authority never fully forms.

You can feel it in meetings. A senior executive pushes a different view and the energy changes. The CMO goes from leading the conversation to explaining why the plan makes sense.

The CMOs who hold real authority do one thing consistently. They anchor every conversation in the metric that keeps the company alive.

Revenue.

Every initiative, every campaign, every hire ties back to revenue impact. Conversations start there and end there. When marketing is clearly framed as a revenue engine, influence stops feeling borrowed. Until that connection is obvious, the CEO ends up acting as the real CMO. Control naturally flows to the person closest to the revenue number.

This dynamic has little to do with ego. It has everything to do with clarity. When marketing’s impact on revenue is obvious, trust grows. When it feels abstract, authority shrinks and someone else steps in.

--- POST 13 ---
Profound raised $96M at a $1B valuation recently.

In last 10 months, the AEO category has grown from 7 tools to over 150. Fifty-four percent of US marketers plan to implement GEO within the next 3-6 months.

The market is racing to track AI visibility. Reasonable response to a real surface shift.

But there is a 46x gap in citation rates across AI platforms for the same brand. Grok cites same brands 27% of the time. ChatGPT cites the same brands 0.59% of the time. Same content, same company in a 30-day window.

So visibility optimisation problem is one thing, the marketers are also going to face an attribution infrastructure problem.

Which platform actually reaches your buyers? That depends on your industry, your buyer persona, your ICP's research behavior.
- Copilot dominates B2B enterprise workflows.
- Perplexity leads in finance.
- ChatGPT drives 77% of AI referral traffic broadly.

The visibility tool tells you where you're cited. It doesn't tell you which citations connect to pipeline for your specific buyers.

The gap isn't solved by optimizing citation rates, which is just a vanity metrics.

It's solved by attribution infrastructure that traces the buyer's full path - where they discovered you, where they engaged, and which surface they converted from.

Marketing ops leaders are buying AI visibility, but none of them will get promoted for telling their Grok citation rate.

What they they need to know is on which surface their last five closed deals started and how they contributed to the Revenue.

--- POST 14 ---
80% of CMO searches end the same way: hired for execution, fired for not being a mind reader.

The job description says launch campaigns, build pipeline, own the funnel. The actual job is knowing which executive to disappoint this week and in what order.

Product needs market education but sales needs leads tomorrow. Finance approved budget for brand but the board wants performance metrics by Friday. The CEO wants category leadership but also wants to hit this quarter's number. Marketing gets all five mandates, zero decision rights about which one wins.

This is why the role burns people out faster than any other executive seat. You're accountable for outcomes but you don't control the inputs. Product ships late, finance cuts budget mid quarter, sales changes ICP twice in six weeks. Marketing still owns the number.

The CMOs who survive aren't the ones with the best playbooks. They're the ones who can walk into a room of executives with conflicting priorities and get them to pick one problem to solve this quarter. Not acknowledge all the problems, pick one. That forces trade offs nobody wants to make, which is why most companies skip this step and just ask marketing to do everything poorly instead of one thing well.

When a CMO gets that alignment, marketing starts working. When they don't, they get six months to prove execution skills that were never the bottleneck.

--- POST 15 ---
Nobody gets promoted for stopping bad marketing.

You get credit for campaigns launched, channels scaled, pipeline generated. You don't get credit for the product positioning that would've failed, the brand campaign that would've burned budget, or the market you didn't enter because the unit economics were fiction.

This is why most VP Marketing hires optimize for velocity over judgment. Boards want to see motion. Teams want to see output. Leadership wants to see momentum in the metrics they're watching. The incentive is always to do more.

But the most valuable marketing work is often deciding what not to do. It's killing the rebrand six weeks in when you realize brand wasn't the problem. It's saying no to the enterprise play when your product isn't ready for procurement cycles. It's telling the CEO their favorite tagline doesn't mean anything to the customer.

That work is invisible until you compare the company that did it to the one that didn't. One has focus, the other has thirty initiatives and no momentum. One has a team that trusts leadership, the other has a team that's learned to ignore strategy because it changes every quarter.

The best marketing leaders I know are defined by what they stopped, not what they started. But nobody writes that job description.

--- POST 16 ---
We just wrapped up our biggest quarter as well as the biggest year at RevSure AI.

In FY2026, we 4x’d our ARR, and in two separate quarters, we added more revenue than we had cumulatively at the start of the year.

What worked for us? We just identified who we're building for. And we went all in. We realized that it's the large enterprises with complex GTM motions - they're the ones who need RevSure AI. The more complex the GTM motion, the faster they recognize the pain. The faster they recognize the pain, the bigger the initial deal. The bigger the initial deal, the faster they expand.

We stopped trying to convince companies with simple motions that they need better attribution. We started finding the ones already operating blind at scale. Those conversations close in weeks, not quarters.

That's when the growth curve broke. Not when we built more features. When we admitted who we're actually built for.

I'm excited for FY2027, it will be the biggest year for RevSure. Again.

--- POST 17 ---
Two weeks ago, $285 billion in SaaS market value got wiped out in a single day.

Anthropic launched Claude Cowork plugins and the market just figured out what operators already know: software that shows you problems is dying to software that solves them.

Between February 3rd and 5th, the S&P Software & Services Index recorded its worst two-day stretch since 2008. Salesforce and Workday are each down over 40% in 12 months.

Every operator I work with stopped asking for dashboards six months ago. Now they all want the same thing: agents that finish the job.

That's what we built at RevSure AI. Agents that craft personalized emails based on a prospect's full journey and LinkedIn profile. Agents that identify high-engagement leads by seniority, industry, company size, and activity signals, then run outreach automatically.

You configure the flow, set the logic, and the agent executes. They use tools like Gmail, SendGrid, or write directly back to Marketo and HubSpot like they're on your team.

A dashboard tells you what happened. An agent does what needs to happen next.

SaaS gave us dashboards. Agents give us leverage.

The $285 billion wipeout wasn't a market correction. It was the market repricing every piece of software that stops at showing you the answer instead of executing it.

We don't need more tools that show us the problem. We need tools that finish the job.

--- POST 18 ---
Last week I posted about Salesforce catering to a newer target segment with the MrBeast ad at the Super Bowl.

The comments were fascinating. But I think the core debate missed the point.

The main pushback was: "Brand loyalty doesn't transfer. Yahoo was every teenager's homepage. Blackberry was the cool kid's phone. Gen Z moves from Snapchat to TikTok to Instagram and back. None of that mattered once enterprise buying started."

That argument is correct. But it completely missed the point.

When Yahoo lost to Google, it wasn't a loyalty problem. When Blackberry lost to Apple, it wasn't a loyalty problem either.

The better product won. That's a product story. Not a brand story.

Most people confuse brand loyalty with product superiority. They're not the same thing. And in enterprise, this distinction matters even more.

Enterprise buyers are not fans. They're operators - budget, stack compatibility, risk, internal optics, procurement friction. Nobody says "I know it's worse, but I love the brand." That's not how six-figure decisions work.

So does brand matter? Yes. Just not the way people think.

Brand doesn't win the deal. Brand gets you on the SHORTLIST.

Before the RFP, the comparison matrix or pricing discussions. There's a quiet filtering moment where teams decide which 3-4 vendors are even worth evaluating.

Nobody spends political capital championing a vendor they've never heard of. And nobody spends it on a vendor that feels like their predecessor's legacy stack or ”Dad's software."

That's the real threat Salesforce is fighting. Becoming Irrelevant. If you're not on that list, your product never gets a chance to prove it's better.

MrBeast doesn't guarantee Salesforce wins the deal. He guarantees they make the list.

TLDR: Product wins the deal. Brand gets you on the shortlist.

--- POST 19 ---
Jason Beddall 10X’d his marketing team’s content output last year. Every efficiency metric went up. Leadership was thrilled.

Then he automated customer outreach, and everything broke. Response rates collapsed, prospects ghosted and buyers could smell the automation from a mile away.

They became more productive and less effective at the same time.

So he drew a line. AI handles everything internal - content production, research, data analysis, ops. But the moment something touches a prospect, it has to be human.

Real understanding of what buyers are dealing with and solving their problem, without any AI automated targeting.

I think a lot of marketing leaders are quietly experiencing this - but not many are talking about it.

We unpacked it on Moneyball CMO, and a few things really stood out:
1. Efficiency ≠ effectiveness
Automation helped with output. It hurt when it replaced human touch in outreach.

2. Building attribution in-house has limits
Jason spent 18 months building. They got to about 65% - then hit a wall.

When he decided to buy RevSure instead of build the remaining 35%, they uncovered $2M in pipeline sitting in their own data. It had been there all along - just fragmented across systems that didn’t talk to each other.

3. Buyer bots are changing the funnel
Bots are filling out forms, downloading whitepapers, and researching vendors - before a human buyer ever steps in.

At one point he said, “One of my least favourite conversations is about who gets credit for what.”

He’s right.

Attribution shouldn’t be about credit. The real question is whether you’re willing to act when the data challenges decisions you’ve already made.

Full breakdown in this week’s Moneyball CMO newsletter (link in comments).

If you’re a CMO challenging conventional wisdom or know one, tag them. We’d love to feature you.

--- POST 20 ---
Building AI is easy. Building AI that earns trust is hard.

When we started RevSure, we knew one thing for sure: if AI is going to sit at the heart of revenue decisions, it has to be responsible, governed, and accountable, not a black box people are afraid of.

RevSure is now ISO/IEC 42001:2023 certified, the world’s first international standard for AI Management Systems.

We’re among the early companies globally to meet this bar. It’s validation of years spent thinking deeply about how our AI is designed, deployed, monitored, and continuously improved.

It also builds on the trust foundation we’ve been quietly putting in place: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001:2022, and GDPR compliance.

Proud of the team. Grateful to our customers. Onward. 🚀

--- POST 21 ---
I have a customer who has developed an 'unhealthy' obsession with our dashboards.

He probably knows more than RevSure than me. So, why's he obsessed?

He got sick of the napkin math he used to do when his CMO or CEO used to ask him what's his projection for this month. He used to put together things, cook up a story and then share the most believable narrative, which also had to be politically correct.

So, when he REALLY got visibility into his pipeline, the first thing he told us was that he'd throw the napkins now. And a month later, he told us that he has developed an unhealthy obsession with his pipeline metrics, and with RevSure.

What changed wasn’t curiosity, it was certainty. When people finally trust the numbers, they stop storytelling and start checking, again and again.

True product adoption doesn't look like usage, it looks like obsession.

--- POST 22 ---
60M people visited MrBeast's Salesforce page during the Super Bowl. Almost none of them will buy enterprise software this quarter.

So "did it work?" is the wrong question.

Salesforce isn't try to buy pipeline, they're buying future preference.

MrBeast's audience is 18-30. Today they're ICs and junior managers. In a few years, many will be VPs choosing CRMs, approving six-figure contracts, building GTM stacks.

That's compound branding.

Most CMOs can't think long-term, not because they lack vision. Because they're playing a political game. They're judged on quarters, pipeline, and narratives they can defend in a boardroom.

I keep seeing CFOs kill brand spend with one question: "What's the pipeline impact this quarter?"

When the CEO personally sponsors the bet, that pressure disappears. The time horizon expands. The politics quiet down.

Salesforce was successful in capturing attention of potential future buyers, because this decision came from the top and gave the org air cover to think long.

That's the real advantage.

Most companies don't have a brand problem. They have a permission problem. And that's the one thing you can't buy with an $8M Super Bowl slot.

--- POST 23 ---
CFOs hate brand spend because brand teams can't speak their language.

"Awareness" doesn't show up in Salesforce.
"Long-term value" doesn't close deals this quarter.

So every budget cycle, brand teams play defense - Trust us. It's building equity. You can't measure everything.

And CFOs despise "trust me" budgets.

We had a customer whose CFO asked what their billboard ROI was. The brand team said "Brand doesn't work like that." CFO said ,"Then we're cutting the budget."

We showed them something different. Not attribution of individual billboard views to individual deals, that's impossible. We showed incrementality: in markets where they ran billboards, pipeline increased 37%. In markets where they didn't, pipeline stayed flat.

Clear comparison - pre and post. CFO language.

The conversation flipped completely. From "prove these billboards work" to "how much more should we spend?"

One customer told us, "That's a very key point in moving that conversation forward and justifying additional spend. If we want to go bigger, what does that look like?"

Same board, same CFO, different question. Brand teams stopped defending their budget and started expanding it.

--- POST 25 ---
A $400M company was starving its best team and overfeeding the other, and they didn't even know about it.

When we told our findings to CMO, she told me that I don't know if I should be angry or happy. Eventually, we grew their pipeline for 105% in 6 months and this was one of the most unique use cases we came across.

So, they had 5 different verticals, and each vertical had different buying cycles, deal sizes, conversion rates. But they compressed everything into averages. And here's the problem with that:

- Average conversion rates mean nothing when one vertical converts at 8% and another converts at 2%
- Average CAC means nothing if cost per opportunity is $6K for one team and $800 for another

Because of this avg play, everyone got the same budget, and that was a disaster. They overfed underperforming teams and channels and underfed champions.

We asked them to reallocate their budgets, they shifted $100K from Team E to Team A. The results were following.
Team A:
- Old budget: $200K - 240 opportunities
- New budget: $300K - 360 opportunities
- New pipeline: +120 opportunities

Team E:
- Old budget: $200K - 30 opportunities
- New budget: $100K - 15 opportunities
- Lost pipeline: -15 opportunities

Net gain: +105 opportunities from the same total budget.

Before RevSure AI, they kept asking for more budget while the real answer was dissecting the averages.

Riddhy Mehta, Alex Cox, Harry Hawk, ⚡️Francisco, Clay Cozby, Brian Cox, Annie Eissler, Glen Shillinglaw, Vivek Gautam, Shantanu, Jayaram, Weisi Kang, Mari Ström, Kathi Kaplan, Alexa Dennett, Ryan Shopp, Dave Kellogg, Tom Wentworth

--- POST 26 ---
We caught a $3M revenue deficit 90 days early and also saved hundreds of thousands of dollars for our customer. It’s still the story I’m most proud of.

The VP of marketing we were working with was spending aggressively on conferences and events, so their top of funnel volume looked great, and their pipeline forecasts were green across the board.

But our attribution infrastructure flagged something which Salesforce didn't, conversion rates were quietly collapsing. So, while their volume was good, their quality was not.

When we followed that signal forward, it was clear they were going to miss revenue targets by $3M, it just hadn’t shown up yet. Revenue lag meant the problem was invisible until it was almost too late to fix.

That’s the danger of summary metrics. You optimize what’s visible, usually volume, while quality erodes unnoticed. By the time it hits revenue, you’re already 90 days behind.

Because we caught it early, the customer pulled the plug on events mid-year and shifted spend to ABM. They still missed the original target, but they avoided having to explain a $3M shortfall with no runway left to recover.

You can’t run your GTM motion with a reactive lens on attribution. Otherwise, you end up digging wells after you’re already thirsty.

--- POST 28 ---
Sam Altman went from calling ads in AI “uniquely unsettling” to saying “we’ll take great care to do it (ads) right.”

This has left CMOs worried about tracking ChatGPT conversations, buyers are anonymous. But that’s not the real problem they’re about to face.

Buyers were already anonymous, more than 95% of B2B website visitors never fill out a form. They were anyway doing research quietly across LinkedIn, your website, competitor sites, review platforms, and G2.

If you can't connect anonymous research to known accounts NOW, ChatGPT doesn't change anything.

Real attribution infrastructure identifies accounts from anonymous activity. That infra is IP resolution, intent signals, firmographic matching, behavioral patterns.

On the other hand, weak attribution waits for form fills.

The solution isn't "ChatGPT-specific tracking." It's attribution infrastructure that identifies buying committees across ANY channel, anonymous or not.

If you built that infrastructure, ChatGPT would become just another signal. If you didn't, ChatGPT would be another excuse for why you can't prove ROI.

A VP of Marketing told me last year that she'd rather use Excel than log into one more dashboard.

Not ours. Any dashboard.

You're building friction between the question and the answer.

The goal wasn't prettier dashboards. It was removing the interface between marketers and the insights they're already paying for.

I showed a CMO how to generate 4x ROI by reallocating $2M from LinkedIn, but he chose to ignore it because "LinkedIn is strategic."

--- POST 34 ---
Making this video has been harder than running RevSure AI.

I'm not a camera person. Never have been. But this felt necessary, because we’re on the edge of real chaos in GTM, and someone needs to say it out loud.

There was a time when B2B GTM was simple. Marketing filled the funnel, sales closed deals, and you could count your tools on one hand. Then things got complicated with more tools, more channels, and more handoffs. By 2020, GTM teams were managing the chaos of tool explosion and fragmented data.

Now we're entering something worse. I call it the Agent Apocalypse.

Everyone's spinning up agents. Inbound agents, outbound agents, prospecting agents, content agents. An army that never sleeps and never talks to each other.

Without shared context, your outbound agent misreads a single click and starts messaging every buyer like they're ready to purchase. Your content agent creates materials that contradict what sales is saying. Your prospecting agent burns leads marketing spent months warming up.

It’s the same handoff and context-loss problem we’ve always had, just happening faster and at scale.

This is why full-funnel visibility isn't optional anymore. If agents are operating across your GTM motion, they need a unified data and context foundation. The teams that will win won’t have the most agents. They’ll have systems of shared context, accountability, and common results.

This video reflects how I see the future of GTM and the problem we’re building to solve at RevSure AI.

--- POST 41 ---
Outreach is valued at $4.4B.
Salesloft raised at valuation of $2.3B.
Apollo.io raised $100M at $1.6B valuation.

Everyone's racing to build AI agents that can write emails, make calls, and book meetings. But here's what nobody's talking about: the handoff problem.
An AI agent can craft a perfect outbound sequence. It can personalize at scale, follow up relentlessly, even book the meeting. But the moment that lead crosses from marketing to SDR to AE, context bleeds at every handoff.

This is the same problem that killed human-driven funnels. And we're about to recreate it with agents.

It's like the Mars Orbiter disaster. One team used metric units, another used imperial. Both were "right" locally. The spacecraft was lost because the boundary didn't share meaning. GTM teams repeat this pattern every quarter.

Marketing's MQL doesn't equal SDR's "accepted" doesn't equal Sales' "qualified." Small semantic drifts compound into pipeline chaos.

Now imagine that chaos multiplied by autonomous agents operating without institutional memory. The agentic era won't be won by the fastest agent builder. It'll be won by whoever solves context preservation across handoffs, human or machine.

--- POST 42 ---
We don’t lose many deals to competitors. We lose them to “we’ll build it ourselves.” And then we win them back.

I’ve seen the pattern enough times to stop arguing. Teams fall in love with the idea of building in-house because the prototype demo looks promising. What they don’t factor in is the ongoing, compounding cost, not just engineering hours but operational complexity.

Three months later, when the system breaks, it’s never just the tech. It’s everything around it: policies, governance, security, legal frameworks, pricing models. With AI, all of these evolve constantly.

Your prototype doesn’t stay a prototype. It becomes a second product, one that demands continuous updates just to remain viable.

“Build” works only when what you are building is the true differentiator. Most of the time, it is maintenance.

And maintenance compounds. Every new requirement, every pivot, every quick fix makes the system more fragile and more expensive to keep alive. By the time the tech is “working,” the business has already moved on.

If you still want to build, be explicit.
- What decisions will you be able to make when it is done?
- Who will own it long-term?
- Can you handle the operational overhead?

If you can’t answer those, don’t ship the prototype. Buy endurance, build edge.

--- POST 43 ---
"Credentials don't matter in AI" is lazy thinking. That narrative protects people from accountability.

The narrative goes, "A+ executives are failing at AI startups while 22-year-olds fine-tuning models in dorms are crushing it. Experience is dead!"

It's a half truth, and completely misdiagnosed. But let's think about who benefits from this narrative?

- The young builder who ships fast and breaks things? "Experience doesn't matter" explains why they don't need to understand enterprise complexity.
- The executive who failed at an AI startup? "The paradigm shifted" explains why their track record didn't translate.
- The investor who backed the wrong team? "Nobody could have predicted this" conveniently eliminates blame.

Everyone gets an excuse and nobody has to be wrong.

But then some executives are succeeding at AI companies, some young builders are failing. The pattern isn't "credentials vs. no credentials."

It's competence vs. incompetence, same as it ever was. The game has definitely changed, but competence still looks like competence:
- understanding problems deeply
- building solutions that work
- earning trust from customers.

“Credentials don’t matter” is comforting to people who don’t want to investigate why they failed. The uncomfortable truth is that in any era, some people figure it out and some don't. The reasons vary, but the outcome doesn't.

--- POST 44 ---
Chaos isn't a pit, chaos is a ladder.

And nowhere is that truer than in data. Software companies love to say “garbage in, garbage out.” It’s their evergreen excuse for why their product fails the moment they meet real enterprise data. But the truth is that real companies do not have clean data. They have a decade of system migrations, shortcuts, naming collisions, abandoned fields, accidental conventions, and improvised workarounds baked into every table.

Good data is a utopian concept, it's not because of intent, it's just because data is a high-entropy discipline. And entropy increases over time, meaning the messiness of your data is the natural state. Data doesn't stay clean, it degrades. Fields get misused, definitions drift, new systems get bolted on. Any system designed for a point-in-time snapshot of data quality will become obsolete as the data evolves.

This is why most enterprise AI implementations fail, they're built on fiction, that someone will "fix the data" before the system goes live. That never happens.
Products which can handle data chaos have an unfair advantage other those who assume perfection in data. Therefore, the only viable product (and pilot) design is one that assumes the mess and builds tolerance for it.

- She's buying for the CEO who wants pipeline visibility.

I'm not selling to her, I'm arming her for battles I'll never see. If you don't understand the battles your champions are fighting, you're handing them a sword when they need a shield. Or vice versa.

The real leverage is helping your champion win a room you’ll never be in.

--- POST 46 ---
No company dies from lack of data. They die when data becomes political instead of analytical.

A CMO told me this a while ago, "As soon as you present a slide that shows a different number than what sales sees, it becomes a debate about the numbers, not a conversation about what you're trying to do." The friction isn't about accuracy. It's about trust.

When the numbers don't match, teams don't fix the funnel & they fight over whose data is right. I call this the "Debate Tax."

And it compounds. Every unresolved dispute breeds more misalignment. Soon, teams stop bringing data to meetings altogether, because they know it'll be challenged. Eventually, the company flies blind by choice. That's more comfortable than fighting. Marketing stops trying to prove ROI because every attempt gets re-litigated.

The irony: the data existed all along. The fragmentation made it weaponizable. When every team has their own dashboard, every team has their own truth. And when everyone has their own truth, nobody has credibility.

This is why we built for the full funnel. When marketing, SDRs, and sales see the same number, the conversation shifts from 'whose data is right' to 'what do we do next.'

--- POST 47 ---
For 1,500 years, everyone knew the Earth was the center of the universe.

Ptolemy's geocentric model worked well enough, it predicted planetary positions. It fit the theology. And then Copernicus heliocentric model showed the sun was the center. His math was better & his predictions were more accurate.

His book got 'restricted' for 100+ years.

Moving from last-touch to multi-touch attribution is your organization's Copernican moment.

You're about to redistribute credit. Channels that looked dominant will shrink. Channels you were about to cut will show up everywhere. And people will fight it. Not because the data is wrong. Because the implications are uncomfortable.

- The head of paid media doesn't want to see organic getting credit.
- The events team doesn't want to share influence with email.
- The VP who championed the big rebrand doesn't want data showing it didn't move the needle.

Your first multi-touch report will be rejected. Plan for it.

- "The methodology must be wrong."
- "We should look at a different time period."
- "Can we rerun the analysis?"

This isn't failure, this is the process. Attribution doesn't fail because of bad math. It fails because executives would rather be comfortable than correct.

The Copernican Revolution wasn't rejected because the math was wrong. It was rejected because it was inconvenient.

Before you implement multi-touch, ask yourself: is your leadership ready to be wrong? If not, you're not buying attribution. You're buying expensive confirmation bias.

A CMO recently asked me: “What part of your Full-Funnel AI are you best at? Most startups only nail one thing.”

Fair question.

Most tools were built to solve tiny slices of the GTM motion — Visitors → leads, leads/Accounts → MQLs/MQLs, MQLs/MQAs → meetings, and so on.

--- POST 57 ---
Inefficient handoffs and missing semantics once cost NASA $125M.

In 1998, the Mars Climate Orbiter was lost because of a unit mismatch. One team sent thruster data in pound-seconds. Another expected newton-seconds.

Both were “right” locally, but wrong globally. The boundary didn’t share meaning, so every course correction nudged the spacecraft slightly off. Over time, those small errors compounded. It entered the Martian atmosphere tens of kilometers too low and contact was lost on arrival day.

Every team had verified their part. None had validated the handoffs.

That’s the real lesson: It wasn’t a calculation error that doomed the Mars Orbiter. It was a handoff failure. GTM teams make the same mistake every quarter.

Marketing’s MQL, SDR’s Accepted, Sales’ Qualified - all are locally consistent, but semantically different. A lead becomes an opportunity without shared context.

These small drifts compound. And you end up missing your quarter.

Funnel definitions are your unit system in GTM funnel. Treat them as contracts, not suggestions. Preserve memory across handoffs. Make boundaries unambiguous.

The disaster never lives in the parts. It lives in the interfaces.
